courses.title: Principles of Arts Administration and Cultural Entrepreneurship
courses.description: Introduces the core principles of arts administration and cultural entrepreneurship as they relate to multiple sectors including music, visual art, dance, theatre, and museums. Explores organizational structure, governance, programming, finances, budgeting, fundraising, and marketing. Includes analysis of real-world arts organizations and the development of a comprehensive strategy plan. Offers students an opportunity to obtain practical experience with arts administration tools such as donor management systems, project management tools, financial software, and social media management platforms. Emphasizes current trends in arts ecosystems including emerging technologies, cultural justice, and economic and environmental sustainability.

courses.title: Arts and Culture Organizational Leadership
courses.description: Offers an overview and introduction to leadership knowledge areas, tools, and skills sets for the arts and culture sector. Key topics include issues and challenges in the management of arts-oriented organizations, leadership characteristics and techniques for arts and culture teams, balancing organizational priorities with artistic vision and values, board formation and management, audience outreach, and operational practices. Focuses on the administration of people and processes to communicate mission; realize goals; and effectively manage the creative resources, human resources, and financial challenges of nonprofit arts and cultural organizations.

courses.title: Planning for Arts and Cultural Organizations
courses.description: Offers an overview and introduction to knowledge areas and primary skills sets for planning, launching, and sustaining arts and cultural organizations. Key topics include evaluating opportunities in the arts and culture sector; building effective vision, mission, and values for arts and culture initiatives in balance with civic and community contexts; smart approaches to arts and culture funding; developing sustainable and flexible strategic plans; and planning challenges for the contemporary strategic arts organization.

courses.title: Information Technology for Arts and Cultural Organizations
courses.description: Offers nontechnical students an opportunity to obtain a clear and current understanding of key information technology (IT) concepts set in the context of arts and cultural organizations and to empower them to make decisions that map technology to strategy. Covers how to identify technical terms, stakeholders, and issues; evaluate IT challenges; apply best-practice frameworks; and identify business needs and compare technical solutions in order to minimize cost and maximize strategic alignment. Combines readings, casework, video lectures, screen casts, guest videos, and a hands-on approach to researching solutions and leading change. Includes both group and individual deliverables that students synthesize to create and present a final project.

courses.title: Advocacy and the Arts
courses.description: Seeks to equip future arts leaders with the competence, power, and commitment to act in the interest of creative resilience—and creativity—for the collective good. Offers students an opportunity to learn how to both advocate for the arts and advocate through the arts. Each module presents a specific challenge faced by artists and arts institutions and compels students to identify and articulate creative solutions to overcome this challenge. Exposes students to diverse knowledge sources—including theoretical and practical literature, organizational and project case studies, and guest presentations by arts leaders in the Boston area—to help prepare students for this important work.

courses.title: Programming and Community Engagement for Cultural Entrepreneurs
courses.description: Examines the role and tools of the cultural entrepreneur and investigates practical and tactical approaches centered around real-world examples. Topics include how cultural entrepreneurs turn new ideas into concrete initiatives and how they communicate with and learn from their audiences and communities to assess and evaluate the implementation of cultural endeavors. Offers students an opportunity to create their own cultural initiative from the ground up. Through modules covering mission and vision, program evaluation, community engagement, and basic resource management, the successful student should finish the course with a real project “in a box,” ready to launch.

courses.title: Building Value Through Cultural Enterprise
courses.description: Examines the question of value through the lens of cultural institutions big and small. Explores examples from real-world case studies. Focuses on areas of value, ways to measure impact on both qualitative and quantitative levels, and how to demonstrate that impact to a variety of audiences from our daily visitors to our federal government. Value in the cultural sector is a critical question that institutions and individuals working in this area must answer on a regular basis for themselves, their constituents, and their supporters.

courses.title: Innovative Approaches to Audience Engagement
courses.description: Investigates the philosophy, methods, and application of a wide spectrum of audience engagement strategies. Utilizes provided materials, inquiry-based research practices, and experiential study to introduce students to the various interpretations and outcomes of effective audience engagement, particularly as it relates to an arts organization’s mission, vision, and values. Drawing directly from their course work and research, students are paired with an arts organization to design a creative audience engagement strategy that both aligns with the organization’s mission and supports a new visionary initiative.

courses.title: Fundraising in the Arts
courses.description: Offers advanced study of fundraising and resource development within the arts and cultural sector. Designed to help students develop the knowledge and skills required to create and increase various revenue streams. Covers fundraising ethics, grant writing, sponsorship proposals, pitching, stakeholder and volunteer engagement, storytelling, and other skills necessary to support fundraising goals. When applicable, students are encouraged to use this course to explore and apply fundraising fundamentals for their organization, startup, or creative practice.

courses.title: Experiential Study in Arts Administration
courses.description: Offers students an opportunity to learn best practices in arts project management, including how to assess and scope a project, develop a timeline with clear action items and goals, relay needs and expectations to clients, research materials to assist in the process, and measure and deliver project results. Faculty coach students to cultivate professional skill sets, build competency around key areas of student interest, and bridge theory with practice. Students receive feedback from their project sponsor, review lessons learned, and incorporate suggestions to improve and further develop their career plans. Seeks to support the development of business communication skills, project and client management skills, and frameworks for analysis.

courses.title: Special Topics in Arts Administration and Cultural Entrepreneurship
courses.description: Focuses on a specific topic of relevance to the domain of arts administration and cultural entrepreneurship. May be repeated up to 5 times for a maximum of 20 semester hours. Topics vary each semester.

courses.title: Financial Reporting for Integrated Multinational Enterprises
courses.description: Provides an in-depth analysis of financial reporting practices for diversified, international business entities. In today’s global business environment, many corporations operate diverse economic activities and often conduct those activities across geographic boundaries. Examines accounting and disclosure standards in the United States that are relevant to presenting consolidated financial statements and analyzes the impact of intercompany transactions. Other topics include hedging, translation, and remeasurement of a foreign subsidiary’s financial statements.

courses.title: Ethics and Public Interest in the Accounting Professions
courses.description: Focuses on the roles and responsibilities of ethics and the public interest in the accounting, auditing, and tax professions. Emphasizes the importance of serving the public interest and meeting professional obligations by educating future accounting professionals on what is the right thing to do and most importantly, how to go about doing it.

courses.title: Special Topics in Accounting
courses.description: Provides an in-depth look at important trends and current issues in the field of accounting. Topics can include developments in the field of auditing, financial accounting, managerial accounting, accounting systems, taxation or corporate governance.

courses.title: Strategic Analytics Leadership and Organizational Transformation
courses.description: Examines advanced leadership frameworks for driving enterprisewide analytical transformation and innovation. Explores competencies in strategic visioning, organizational change management, and the creation of analytics-driven cultures at scale. Offers students an opportunity to design and evaluate comprehensive data governance ecosystems, lead cross-functional transformation initiatives, and develop original frameworks for sustainable competitive advantage through analytics. Emphasizes the executive leader's role in balancing data-driven insights with strategic intuition, stakeholder dynamics, and ethical considerations in complex organizational environments.

courses.title: Enterprise Decision Intelligence and Strategic Business Systems
courses.description: Explores the strategic design and leadership of enterprisewide decision intelligence systems that integrate artificial intelligence, business intelligence, and human judgment for competitive advantage. Offers students an opportunity to develop competencies in architecting comprehensive decision support ecosystems, leading digital transformation initiatives, and creating innovative approaches to automated and augmented decision making. Emphasizes the executive perspective on balancing technological capabilities with organizational readiness, regulatory compliance, and stakeholder trust.

courses.title: Technology Strategy and Digital Innovation Leadership
courses.description: Offers nontechnical executives leading data-driven organizations an opportunity to advance their competencies in technology strategy, digital innovation management, and technical team leadership. Students develop frameworks for evaluating emerging technologies, managing technology portfolios, and leading digital transformation initiatives. Emphasizes strategic decision making around technology architecture, vendor selection, and innovation investment while building organizational technical capabilities. Explores research on technology adoption patterns, innovation management, and digital transformation effectiveness.

courses.title: Global Sustainable Urban Design Sprint
courses.current_step: Provost Continuing Education Module Oversight Group
courses.description: Introduces sustainable design and innovation in majority-world cities facing challenges related to population growth, health and well-being, and climate-driven extreme weather. Uses a time-bound design sprint framework to explore systems thinking and design approaches applied to global climate resilience. Emphasizes ethical design, collaborative problem-solving, and cross-cultural perspectives, with opportunities for engagement with professionals working in the field.

courses.title: Design Studio I
courses.description: These courses deal with our relationship to space, form and the environment in a broad sense. Emphasis is on human factors, scale, materials and structures. The courses progress from abstract to problem-solving through analysis and include consideration of professional design problems of modern scope. Offered by Pratt Institute as part of the CAMD Global Creativity Contexts Program.

courses.title: Design II
courses.description: Studies our relationship to space, form, and the environment in a broad sense. Emphasizes human factors, scale, materials, and structures. Progresses from abstract concepts to problem solving through analysis and considers professional design problems of modern scope. Offered by Pratt Institute as part of the CAMD Global Creativity Contexts Program.

courses.title: Assemblies and Environments
courses.description: Assemblies and Environments is an introduction to the principles and practices applied to the constructed environment, providing an overview of their materials, components, assemblies, environmental systems, and construction theory. Through weekly lectures on topics related to materials and systems of the built environment, the course examines the anatomy of a building and how building systems are coordinated with and impact the design of the interior. Through a study of historical precedents, this course examines how, the evolution of materials, technologies and integrated systems have impacted design solutions. Beyond an introduction to the various building systems-including structural, mechanical, plumbing, and electrical-the course will explore how acoustic, lighting and thermal design impacts the quality of the interior environment. Offered by Pratt Institute as part of the CAMD Global Creativity Contexts Program.

courses.title: Modern Architecture
courses.description: Considers the forms and principles—as well as the sources for and development of—architecture and urbanism during the twentieth century. Explores the paradoxes within what has broadly been termed modernism, including the tension between historicism and innovation; between universal principles and regional expressions; between industry and craft; and between the utopian vision of planners and the role of individual will.

courses.title: Color, Materials, Lighting
courses.description: Offers an overview of the fundamentals of the interior environment (color, materials, and lighting). Examines their interactions in impacting interior spaces and explores their functional and aesthetic properties. Builds upon the foundation of color theory to study those principles with respect to the interior environment. Offered by Pratt Institute as part of the CAMD Global Creativity Contexts Program.

courses.title: Language of Drawing I
courses.description: Language of Drawing I introduces students to the communication of physical and conceptual ideas of space, materiality, light and construction. Emphasis will be places on developing methods of working across media, understanding the appropriate form and means of representation to develop literacy and resourcefulness in design drawing and communication. Offered by Pratt Institute as part of the CAMD Global Creativity Contexts Program.

courses.title: Language of Drawing II
courses.description: Emphasizes the representation of spatial, material, and sensual elements in existing, proposed, and remembered environments. Focuses on using drawing and representation tools and techniques as a means to analyze, distill, and present complex information and ideas. Encourages students to develop and refine a personal representational language, building upon an understanding of graphic conventions. Stresses the clarity and the expressive qualities of graphic communication applied to concepts of interior design. Offered by Pratt Institute as part of the Global Creativity Contexts Program.

courses.title: Design Studio III
courses.description: Students study the design of large-scale spaces with an emphasis on planning institutional public and commercial spaces (contract design work). The courses include an exploration of complete solutions to environmental, interior and architectural problems based on space analysis and planning. Coordination of furnishings, materials, user needs and equipment is emphasized. Offered by Pratt Institute as part of the CAMD Global Creativity Contexts Program.

courses.title: Design IV
courses.description: Studies the design of large-scale spaces with an emphasis on planning institutional public and commercial spaces (contract design work). Explores complete solutions to environmental, interior, and architectural problems based on space analysis and planning. Emphasizes coordination of furnishings, materials, user needs, and equipment. Offered by Pratt Institute as part of the CAMD Global Creative Contexts Program.

courses.title: Time, Culture, Context
courses.description: Introduces students to psychological, social, and cultural approaches to design through readings, discussions, and assignments. Readings explore issues of identity, home, public and private, and ethics. Offered by Pratt Institute as part of the CAMD Global Creativity Contexts Program.

courses.title: Materials, Methods, Meaning
courses.description: Through readings, discussions and assignments, this course introduces students to formal and material approaches to design and construction. Readings explore issues of aesthetics, form-making, materiality, spatial and atmospheric experience, decoration, and details. Writing and discussion will consider the design and fabrication processes, and how attitudes and interpretations of interior spaces are dynamic and changing. Offered by Pratt Institute as part of the CAMD Global Creativity Contexts Program.

courses.title: History of Interior Design
courses.description: A historical overview of the styles and social movements which preceded and laid the foundation for the contemporary practice of interior design, i.e. Regency, Federal, Pennsylvania Dutch, Shaker, Victorian, Art Deco, etc. It includes an examination of the trends in related design areas - architecture, landscape, design, city and town planning. Major emphasis is placed on more modern developments, between 1860 to the present. In addition, a correlated review of the design of furniture, fabrics, accessories, and illumination are examined. Required for majors in Interior Design. Offered by Pratt Institute as part of the CAMD Global Creativity Contexts Program.

courses.title: Modern Chinese Architecture
courses.description: Covers the development of the built environment in China from 1840 to the present. Emphasizes educational and professional shifts in architectural practice, political engagement in the design process, structural and technological transformation, conceptual background, and global impact.

courses.title: Advanced Architectural Communication
courses.description: Offers students an opportunity to build on their representation skills to develop ability in design communication and strategies for effective presentation of analog and digital material.

courses.title: Emergent Platforms
courses.description: Emergent Platforms introduces students to digital drawing, modeling and fabrication techniques, exploring the relationships between design, tools, and construction processes enabled through evolving digital platforms. Integral to the explorations will be an understanding of applicable materials-their effects, potentials, and constraints. Students will explore potential of digital technologies applied to various scales and applications across disciplines including furniture and objects as well as environmental constructs. Offered by Pratt Institute as part of the CAMD Global Creativity Contexts Program.

courses.title: Topics in Architecture Design
courses.description: Offers study of topics in architecture design. Topics vary by semester and by section. Taught by faculty according to their interests and expertise. Please consult department for current offerings. May be repeated up to three times.

courses.title: Special Projects
courses.description: Coursework/and or projects that are assigned on an individual basis. Offered by Pratt Institute as part of the CAMD Global Creativity Contexts Program.

courses.title: Urban and Architectural History Abroad
courses.description: Offers an on-site study of architecture and urban history conducted abroad. Instructors accompany students to visit and lecture about the most significant sites in the history of architecture, art, and urban development of a specific country. In comparison to a traditional on-campus course, the number of examples covered is smaller; however, each example is discussed in much greater detail. Encourages students to discover problems and aspects in art, architecture, and urbanism that have not been raised before, something only possible through direct survey and observation. Offers students an opportunity to obtain a real sense of architectural research without neglecting the basics of the field. Interactions with practicing architects, city planners, policymakers, preservationists, museum professionals, and artists are integral parts of this course.

courses.title: Structural Systems
courses.description: Introduces the fundamental concepts of structural analysis and design for architecture. Examines the nature of forces and their effects on different types of structural elements; the structural properties of shapes and materials; and the selection, analysis, and design of efficient structural systems that resist the loads acting upon them. Uses historical and contemporary examples to illustrate how the changing context of architectural ideas drives structural form and the selection of structural systems. Includes field trips and student presentations of structural models and diagrams. Restricted to students in the architecture BS program and to students in the three-year MArch program.

courses.title: Case Studies in Urban Development
courses.description: Introduces students to the comprehensive range of factors─public investment, financing, regulations, and politics─that influence the programming and design of large-scale urban redevelopment projects. Examines the people and communities impacted by large-scale development and explores how their voices shape the planning and approvals process.

courses.title: Character Animation for Games, Film and Immersive Media
courses.description: Studies kinematic systems and human physiology to simulate bipedal motion with the goal of believable animated performance grounded in anatomy and physics. Explores how key principles of animation are grounded in physical reality and biomechanics. Studies how emotional change alters movement. Presents an in depth study of how to use timing and spacing in the creation of character animation. Offers hands on experience creating animated performances individually or as a group.

courses.title: Photography: Visual Strategies + Context
courses.description: Explores the potential of sequential art forms for poetic, personal, and documentary expression. Students will survey a variety of interdisciplinary approaches to sequential storytelling with a focus on photo-animations and photographic books. Through lectures, readings, screenings, and discussions students will learn to recognize and identify strategies for creating visual metaphors and conceptual montages. Students will then apply this knowledge to create their own books, animations, and multimedia projects that reflect their own personal visions. Field trips to local libraries and museums will provide inspiration for creative projects and opportunities for students to contextualize their own work within historic and contemporary photographic practices.

courses.title: Elements of Visual Composition Tools
courses.description: Introduces 2D art and design software including Adobe Photoshop, Adobe Illustrator, and Adobe InDesign.

courses.title: Making with Form and Materials Tools
courses.description: Introduces a range of tools and industry-standard software for creating 3D forms and making. Offers students an opportunity to learn about file setup and safe operation of basic wood shop tools, CNC laser cutters, and 3D printers. Focuses on the unique affordance of each tool to enable students to creatively experiment with these processes. Covers the basics of 3D modeling and vector drawing and making.

courses.title: Making with Video, Sound, and Animation Tools
courses.description: Focuses on developing an understanding of, and dexterity with, movement- and time-based media and software—including After Effects—to create motion graphics, short animations, video, and montage-style films.

courses.title: Designing Interactive Experiences Tools
courses.description: Introduces wireframing and industry-standard tools and software for creating basic web-based content using HTML and CSS. Offers students an opportunity to create a portfolio website for showcasing projects created in ARTF 1250.

courses.title: Product Design and Human Impact
courses.description: Offers students an integrative opportunity to learn product design from a combined technical and human perspective. Topics studied include a historical view of how technology has shaped society, how society has understood the role of technology, and how the values of the designer affect the technologies that are developed for the human experience. Requires students to identify a human need and to design and prototype a product solution. Students will present their work including placement of the problem and proposed solution in a human context.

courses.title: Information Design Principles
courses.description: Introduces foundational concepts, methods, and procedures for the creation of data-rich information graphics. Investigates and implements visual systems and information structures such as maps, graphs, infographics, charts, and diagrams. Explores conceptual and visual solutions, and the creative process of organizing, visualizing, and communicating information. Examines design solutions that make complex information easier to understand and use. Course experiences include: analysis of design case studies and other critical readings, lectures, class discussions, and individual in-studio design work.

courses.title: Graphic Design Principles
courses.description: Introduces foundational graphic design principles, processes, and methods for creating meaning within function, content, and context. Covers graphic form and vocabulary. Uses typography; language; image; symbolism; and visual principles of composition, hierarchy, rhythm, balance, scale, texture, pattern, grid, value, and color to create effective visual communication. Analyzes design case studies and offers critical readings, lectures, class discussions, and individual in-studio design work.

courses.title: Fashion and Wearable Technologies
courses.description: Introduces key social, environmental, and technical challenges underpinning sustainable innovation in the fashion, wearable technology, and smart textiles industries. Examines inclusive design and circular economy principles and uses them as a framework within which to analyze complex socio-technical networks of production, use and disposal of fashion, wearable technology, and smart textile products, and their impacts on health and wellbeing, society and the planet. Presents the opportunity to gain firsthand experience of the complex nature of today's open, dynamic, and networked problems through a site visit, case studies, and a series of practical activities. Instills understanding of creative intelligence and innovation as a field of practice.

courses.title: Interaction Design Principles
courses.description: Introduces foundational interaction design principles, processes, and tools for creating physical and screen-based interfaces. Uses design research methods, stakeholder identification, problem framing, information structuring, low- to high-fidelity prototyping techniques, iterative development, assessment strategies, and usability testing to construct design proposals for interactive artifacts. Analyzes design case studies and offers critical readings, instructor and guest lectures, class discussions, and individual and collaborative in-studio design work.

courses.title: Creative Fabrication Design Principles
courses.description: Introduces theoretical, methodological, and practical approaches to transforming physical products into meaningful, contextual experiences. Analyzes real-world examples through observation, reverse engineering, and critical discussion, identifying technical and cultural factors that shape impactful interactions between environments, products, and people. Fieldwork at a major global design event forms a central component, fostering direct engagement with contemporary design practices. Emphasizes accessibility, meaning making, sustainability, and social impact, exploring their resonance across users, communities, and stakeholders. Integrates theory and practice through field research and applied design projects, culminating in comprehensive analysis, innovative reporting tools, the application of meta-design principles to design guidelines for future product-related experiences, and the creation of frameworks for others to design.

courses.title: Physical and Digital Fabrication
courses.description: Explores interdisciplinary projects and themes in immersive media and physical making by fabricating novel artifacts and experiences. Students form groups to create design solutions to wicked problems. Student teams follow a hackathon model to explore multiple ideas quickly. By engaging in critique and studio practice, offers students an opportunity to demonstrate and grow their technical skills.

courses.title: Typography 2
courses.description: Continues ARTG 2250, exploring structures and hierarchies through increasing typographic complexity. Investigates meaning, legibility, and readability with an emphasis on voice, organization, sequence, and the typographic grid.

courses.title: Identity and Brand Design
courses.description: Addresses the origins, significance, and consequence of identity and branding expressions, in diverse media, in terms of personal, cultural, and commercial values. Using design research and studio methods, a series of exercises explores expressions of individual and collective identity. Offers students an opportunity to work in teams to develop branding projects in a process designed to increase their capacity to create effective brand expressions and analyze semiotic significance and cultural and economic value. Critique of work and presentation of concepts of identity and brand seek to sharpen students’ skills and challenge their ideas about brand. External critique seeks to create valuable tests of bias and assumptions, while principles of managing attention and trust seek to build the ability to function as a brand steward in actual practice.

courses.title: Experience Design Principles
courses.description: Introduces foundational experience design principles, processes, and methods for understanding and creating conditions and interventions that impact people’s experiences. Uses design research methods, stakeholder identification, visual synthesis of research findings, and problem framing as means to understand the motivations, behaviors, and values of audiences and participants. Develops scenarios and prototypes to construct design proposals informing people’s relationships and experiences with products, environments, and services. Analyzes design case studies and offers critical readings, lectures, class discussions, and individual in-studio design work.

courses.title: Topics in Typographic Inquiry
courses.description: Builds upon basic typographic skills and knowledge to extend, expand, and deepen the student’s understanding of the full range of possibilities for typographic expression as text, image, and experience of a reader. Challenges students to engage with letterform across historical and cultural practices of writing in a wide range of media. Examines how to identify the derivation and significance of writing styles by analyzing typographic form, composition, and presentation to the reader in a variety of contexts. Requires students to experiment with alphabets, fonts, and letterform as systems of signs with semiotic, semantic, logical, grammatical, and syntactical possibilities. Employs conceptual thinking, observation, analysis, and synthesis to devise and implement original ideas in various media.

courses.title: Techniques for Data Visualization
courses.description: Presents computational methods for creating intricate data visualizations through ludic simulations. Implements computational simulations of bodies, particle systems, and natural systems inspired by the behavior of natural systems such as ant colonies, flocking birds, and slime mold behavior. Employs a hands-on learning approach in which students build visualizations from the ground up using these techniques. Introduces fundamental principles of data visualization and applies those principles to specific datasets. Covers essential programming concepts including functions, class instantiation, and the use of arrays and array lists.

courses.title: Complexity in Systems
courses.description: Introduces students to systems thinking and future-oriented mindsets as tools for addressing today’s sustainability challenges. Explores complex problems through a systems-based perspective by applying the practical application of mindset tools such as loop analysis, anticipatory thinking, identifying leverage points,and preparing for change and uncertainty. Emphasizes project work, case studies, and in-class activities for students to develop strategic, interpersonal, and values-driven skills while learning to navigate complexity with creativity and care. Recognizes the interconnected frameworks of complex challenges within planetary boundaries, and applies systems, future, and values thinking to propose innovative and sustainable interventions in complex real-world settings.

courses.title: Advanced Computational Techniques for Data Visualization
courses.description: Explores advanced computational and algorithmic approaches to data visualization through the study and implementation ludic simulated systems for creating intricate data visualizations. Students design and implement computational simulations including particle systems, agent-based models, and bio-inspired algorithms that draw from natural systems such as swarm intelligence, flocking behavior, and cellular automata. The course emphasizes theoretical foundations alongside practical application, requiring students to critically evaluate contemporary research in computational visualization and generative design. Students develop custom visualization frameworks from foundational principles, applying them to domain-specific datasets while engaging with current literature on information aesthetics and perceptual cognition. Advanced programming concepts including object-oriented design and algorithmic optimization are integrated throughout.

courses.title: Human Centered AI
courses.description: Explore the foundations of Human-Centered AI in this dynamic graduate course. Examine the critical processes underpinning AI development and deployment. Investigate methodologies for integrating human perspectives into AI systems. Apply user-centered design principles to AI-driven products and services. Analyze the ethical implications of AI applications in various organizational contexts. Discuss strategies for fostering collaboration between AI technologies and human users. Evaluate case studies showcasing the diverse uses of AI across business processes and products. Engage in hands-on projects to design AI solutions tailored to human needs. Critically assess the societal impacts of Human-Centered AI adoption. Prepare to contribute to the responsible use of AI systems that prioritize human values and well-being.

courses.title: Thesis
courses.description: Offers students support in developing and producing the written component of a design thesis that integrates and applies their accumulated knowledge. Encourages student participation within a practice and research community consisting of classmates, advisor(s), and external professionals. May be repeated twice for a maximum of 12 semester hours.

courses.title: Thesis Project and Exhibition
courses.description: Offers students support in developing and producing thesis project and exhibition that integrates and applies their accumulated knowledge. Encourages student participation within a practice and research community consisting of classmates, advisor(s), and external professionals. Prereq.Experience design and Information design and visualization students only.

courses.title: Visual Intelligence
courses.description: Examines via interdisciplinary lectures how image technologies and techniques of perception endemic to visual art, popular culture and digital media shape how visual culture is understood within an expanding knowledge economy. Introduces analytical skills of observation and methods of contextual analysis (materialism, semiotics, feminisms, LGBTQ studies, queer theory, theories of decolonization and disability studies), in order for students to develop compelling interpretations of visual phenomena within a shifting global context. Visual Intelligence explains how visual studies connects to the fields of law, design, publishing, curating, conservation, and other areas of knowledge production.

courses.title: Seminar in Visual Intelligence
courses.description: Accompanies ARTH 1001. Fosters in-depth discussion, allows for hands-on workshops and facilitates visits to area museums and cultural organizations. Emphasizes the ways digital image technologies are socially constructed and are based on earlier paradigms of classification and differentiation. Seminar meetings demonstrate how to critically read a range of images (e.g. texts, films, videogames, memes, artworks) by paying attention to the ways meaning is often shaped by identarian formations such as race, gender, sexual orientation, class, and ability. Investigates how diverse perspectives enhance the ability to create, while introducing students to creative professionals who actively use visual intelligence in their dynamic careers.

courses.title: History of Animation
courses.description: This course examines the international history of animation, its many influences, practitioners, techniques, and historical periods. Our class will develop the analytical tools to closely analyze animated films and to understand their impact on society and global culture. The course emphasizes the theory and critical study of animation. Readings, lectures, and discussion will be accompanied by in-class film screenings of animated films (ie. early animation, animated shorts, animated features, television animation, computer animation, etc.) and visits by animation artists, practitioners, and industry professionals.

courses.title: Biomechanics
courses.description: Designed to acquaint students with concepts of stress, strain, and constitutive laws as applied to problems in biomechanics. Introduces rigid body and deformable body mechanics. Focuses on basic foundations of solid mechanics using vectors and tensors. Illustrative examples from tissue and cell biomechanics are given where appropriate.

courses.title: Quantitative Physiology for Bioengineers
courses.description: Introduces engineering and science students to core knowledge and understanding of physiological systems and processes. Focuses on quantitative analysis of human physiological systems. Topics include the physical and chemical foundations of physiology; coupled forces and flows; electrical, mechanical, and chemical potentials and their conjugated fluxes; and the physiology of excitable tissue. Examines cell structure, function, and homeostasis with a particular focus on membrane transport, osmotic pressure, cell signaling, and cellular energetics.

courses.title: Bioelectricity
courses.description: Discusses principles of electrical circuit theory and analysis methods for understanding and designing biomedical devices and biological sensors. Covers basic circuit components, network analysis methods, RC and RL circuits, AC circuit analysis, equivalent circuits, biopotential amplifiers, and biopotential electrodes. Introduces biosensing, biosignal processing, and operating principles of electrically active cells and tissues.

courses.title: Transport and Fluids for Bioengineers
courses.description: Covers the fundamental principles of processes and systems in which mass, energy, and momentum are transported in typical biological problems. Emphasizes momentum transport for incompressible and compressible fluids (fluid flow) and energy transport. The methods taught are relevant to the analysis of physiological systems, processing, and separation of biological materials.

courses.title: Biomolecular Dynamics and Control
courses.description: Focuses on the principles of thermodynamics and kinetics applied to the analysis and design of biomolecular systems. Covers foundational topics—such as mass and energy balances, chemical equilibria, and enzyme kinetics—in a biological context. Introduces the role of feedback and feed-forward control in biomolecular networks, emphasizing basic analytical and computational methods, including the use of MATLAB, for analyzing how these regulatory structures affect the dynamics of small-scale, prototypical networks.

courses.title: Foundational Mathematics for Engineers
courses.description: Offers students an opportunity to enhance mathematical proficiency in preparation for more advanced courses in engineering. Focuses on key topics such as algebra, calculus, linear algebra, and ordinary and partial differential equations.

courses.title: Foundational Applied Mathematics for Bioengineers
courses.description: Covers and emphasizes practical applications of linear algebra and differential equations in the field of bioengineering. Explores analytical and numerical solution methods for solving differential equations and applies signal processing techniques for biomedical applications.

courses.title: Computational Tools in Bioengineering
courses.description: Introduces programming and essential numerical methods with applications in bioengineering. Covers scripting, plotting, matrix operations, root-finding techniques, numerical integration, and solving ordinary differential equations. Focuses on developing practical problem-solving skills.

courses.title: Cellular Engineering
courses.description: Analyzes the techniques that form the foundation of molecular cell engineering, including recombinant DNA, cloning and genomics, prokaryotic and eukaryotic gene regulation and single-cell gene expression, structure, dynamics of gene regulatory networks, metabolism and cellular energetics, cell structure, cytoskeleton and cellular motors, synthetic gene circuits, and metabolic engineering.

courses.title: Design of Implants
courses.description: Utilizes an interdisciplinary approach to evaluate how a team designs medical implants to meet unmet clinical needs. Covers bioelectronic medicine, optogenetics, lymphatic system, cardiovascular, neurodegenerative mitigation, tissue engineering, orthopedics, failed medical device implants case studies, ASTM medical device standards, and human factors. Presents exogenous factors including intellectual property, regulatory requirements, affordability, and bioethics. The class is divided into teams that design proprietary implantable medical devices. Presents case studies by experts with firsthand experience in meeting unmet market needs.

courses.title: Research
courses.description: Offers students an opportunity to conduct full-time research under faculty supervision.

courses.title: Research
courses.description: Offers students an opportunity to conduct full-time research under faculty supervision. May be repeated without limit.

courses.title: Lab for BIOL 1107
courses.description: Accompanies BIOL1107. Includes various lab experiments that emphasize evolutionary principles, cellular structure and function, genetic transmission, energy pathways, and physiology.

courses.title: Lab for BIOL 1111
courses.description: Accompanies BIOL 1111. Offers students an opportunity to collect quantitative data through hands-on experimentation as well as simulations. Data is analyzed statistically and presented in written form.

courses.title: Lab for BIOL 1113
courses.description: Accompanies BIOL 1113. Covers topics from the course through various experiments.

courses.title: Lab for BIOL 2217
courses.description: Accompanies BIOL 2217. Covers topics from the course through various experiments.

courses.title: Lab for BIOL 2219
courses.description: Accompanies BIOL 2219. Covers topics from the course through various experiments.

courses.title: Lab for BIOL 2221
courses.description: Accompanies BIOL 2221. Covers topics from the course through various experiments.

courses.title: Inquiries in Biological Sciences
courses.description: Focuses on the latest developments in the field. Offers students an opportunity to explore both scientific practice and progress through readings, discussion, and projects and to expand and deepen their understanding of fundamental biological principles.

courses.title: Lab for BIOL 2301
courses.description: Accompanies BIOL 2301. Reinforces and extends concepts presented and practiced in the accompanying lecture course through the application of scientific investigation methods and data analysis.

courses.title: Biology Project Lab
courses.description: Offers an inquiry-based, intensive laboratory experience in which students have an opportunity to design and conduct independent research projects, applying approaches and techniques used in cell and molecular biology. Offers students an opportunity to present their results in professional formats.

courses.title: Principles of Genomics
courses.description: Explores how access to complete genome sequences has transformed our understanding of heredity, gene function, and evolution. Students are introduced to standard concepts, techniques, and lines of inquiry used in genomic research while developing computational literacy through hands-on work with genome browsers, sequence databases, and standard genomics file formats. Topics include genome sequencing and assembly, transcriptomics, variation, genome-wide association studies, and comparative genomics. The course emphasizes critical evaluation of genomic data sources and prepares students for upper-level genomics and bioinformatics coursework and research opportunities in computational biology.

courses.title: Lab for BIOL 3421
courses.description: Accompanies BIOL 3421. Covers topics from the course through various experiments.

courses.title: Lab for BIOL 3611
courses.description: Accompanies BIOL 3611. Covers topics from the course through various experiments.

courses.title: Molecular Cell Biology for Biotechnology
courses.current_step: BIOL Graduate Department Curriculum Committee Chair
courses.description: Integrates biochemistry and molecular biology in the cellular context. Includes the organization and replication of genomes, principles and methods for genetic manipulation, the regulation of gene expression, and the structure and function of organelles. Emphasizes protein synthesis, including translation, post-translational modifications, and translocations of proteins within the cells and secretion.

courses.title: Independent Research
courses.current_approver_emails: a.francois@northeastern.edu; m.giannini@northeastern.edu; c.mattos@northeastern.edu; b.nelson@northeastern.edu; m.rubock@northeastern.edu; x.tan@northeastern.edu
courses.description: Offers independent work under the direction of members of the department on a chosen topic. Course content depends on the instructor. May be repeated once for a maximum of 4 semester hours.

courses.title: The Role of Patents in the Biotechnology Industry, Past and Future
courses.description: Covers the basics of patenting and the application of patents to the biotechnology industry, including the controversial area of gene patents.

courses.title: Drug Safety and Immunogenicity
courses.description: Introduces the fundamental molecular interactions involved in immunological responses as well as in measuring and testing in a research and regulated environment. Other drug-safety-related topics include adventitious agents (viruses, microorganisms, mycoplasma) and risk factors such as product-related substances (aggregates and post-translationally modified variants), endotoxins, DNA, host-cell proteins, process contaminants such as antibiotics, and the means of testing and removing these through validated processes.

courses.title: Bioprocess Fundamentals
courses.description: Focuses on the fundamental principles and elements in the process of manufacturing biopharmaceuticals. Covers kinetics of enzymatic reactions; selected microbial and cell metabolism and relevant control mechanisms; kinetics of cell growth, cell death, substrate consumption, and product formation; mathematical modeling and representation of bioprocesses; examples of industrial bioprocesses to illustrate types and operations of upstream and downstream unit operations and mass transfers in fermentation systems—the affecting factors and the impact on process development and scale-up. Also includes an overview of economic considerations. Emphasizes bioprocesses for recombinant protein production.

courses.title: Molecular Interactions of Proteins in Biopharmaceutical Formulations
courses.description: Offers an up-to-date survey and review of the research and understanding of the molecular interactions of proteins in biopharmaceutical formulations, including both liquid and solid formats, during the process of drug product manufacturing. Focuses on protein-protein interactions, protein-excipients (e.g., stabilizers, surfactants) interactions, and protein at interface surfaces interactions that are critical and impactful on the stability and integrity of therapeutic proteins of interest. Emphasizes understanding the mechanistic aspect of the interactions; the approaches, methods, and techniques employed to study these phenomena; and measures considered to modulate such interactions to enhance the performance of the biopharmaceutical formulations. Students who do not meet course prerequisites may seek permission of instructor.

courses.title: Cutting-Edge Applications in Molecular Biotechnology
courses.description: Introduces the uses of molecular biology in a biotechnology setting. Includes a brief review of the basics and then dives into state-of-the-art molecular biology applications used in biotechnology today. These applications include stability and expression of cloned gene products, gene cloning strategies, transgenic species, mutation creation and analysis, DNA fingerprinting, PCR technology, microarray technology, gene probes, gene targeting, gene therapy, stem cell technology, antisense RNA, CAR T-cell therapy, RNA interference, and CRISPR/Cas9.

courses.title: Vaccines and Immunization
courses.description: Emphasizes the importance of using vaccines in order to prevent the spread of infectious diseases and viruses. Discusses the evolution of vaccines throughout medical history (e.g., smallpox, polio), emphasizing lessons learned so they can be adapted to development of new and novel vaccines. Explains the molecular mechanism of how vaccines, such as vaccines that use attenuated virus to more novel vaccines that use mRNA, elicit an immune response. Examines the importance of immunization in the context of the immune system.

courses.title: Molecular Tools for Vaccine Design
courses.description: Discusses the molecular tools used for vaccine design and development, including the experimental tools used for attenuated whole-organism vaccines, purified macromolecule vaccines, and DNA or mRNA vaccines. Emphasizes vector design and development and specifically the challenges associated with vaccine design and development. Also studies computational tools that aid vaccine development.

courses.title: Biotech Fundamentals for Apprentices
courses.current_step: Provost Continuing Education Module Oversight Group Hold
courses.description: Introduces the integrated science of genomics, proteomics, and bioinformatics using a case study, hands-on, problem-solving approach. Additionally, learners practice cutting-edge skills and techniques used in research labs and biopharmaceutical companies, including preparing solutions and media, culturing cells, setting up and using a bioreactor, and performing assays including electrophoresis, protein quantification and DNA extraction.

courses.title: Biotech Laboratory Skills
courses.current_step: Provost Continuing Education Module Oversight Group
courses.description: Introduces the biotechnology industry and foundational laboratory practices used in life science research and manufacturing. Provides hands-on experience in a BSL-2 laboratory environment and reviews essential laboratory safety protocols for working with biological materials. Covers core competencies such as sterile technique, contamination control, and standard operating procedures used across life science laboratories. Explores industry challenges and career pathways while developing practical skills applicable to a range of research and production settings.

courses.title: First-Year Career Accelerator
courses.description: Connects first-year business students with upper-class peer mentors to build essential career readiness skills. Explores industry career paths via alumni conversations, develop strategic networking approaches, and create polished professional materials. Emphasizes community building while providing personalized guidance on resume development, LinkedIn optimization, and professional goal-setting. Builds confidence navigating college while building meaningful connections with peers and mentors, creating a strong foundation for co-op and internship success. Prepares students to strategically connect their academic studies with industry opportunities.

courses.title: Strategic Sustainability
courses.description: Studies the three dimensions (environmental, social, and governance) of strategic sustainability. Introduces tools and frameworks for analyzing a firm’s sustainability and facilitating its strategic sustainability transformation. Examines what strategic sustainability is and why it is important; the drivers of strategic sustainability; measurement of strategic sustainability; and implementation of environmental, social, and governance strategic sustainability. Offers students an opportunity to analyze and provide recommendations on how to implement a firm’s strategic sustainability.

courses.title: Scholarly Project Continuation
courses.description: Offers continued registration while completing scholarly project to meet program requirements in applied psychology.

courses.title: Systematic Inquiry in Behavior Analysis 1
courses.description: Offers peer and individualized mentorship to complete a scholarly behavior analytic project proposal. Students identify a behavioral problem and research question, propose a project-type format and methods, and assess the need for IRB application approval and subsequent application.

courses.title: Systematic Inquiry in Behavior Analysis 2
courses.description: Continues CAEP 7412. Offers peer and faculty review and mentorship to complete a scholarly behavior analytic project. Students write a report detailing completion of a systematic literature review, correlational case study, or systematic replication of a published experimental study.

courses.title: Experiential Learning in Skill Acquisition
courses.description: Offers students an opportunity to obtain practical experience developing, implementing, and evaluating skill acquisition programs while working or volunteering in a behavior analytic setting. Provides optional supervision toward accrual of concentrated supervised fieldwork hours required to sit for the Board Certified Behavior Analyst ® examination.

courses.title: Experiential Learning in Behavioral Supervision
courses.description: Offers students an opportunity to practice methods of staff training and supervision while working or volunteering in behavior analytic settings. Provides optional supervision toward accrual of concentrated supervised fieldwork hours required to sit for the Board Certified Behavior Analyst ® examination.

courses.title: Thinking Analytically with Data
courses.current_step: Provost Continuing Education Module Oversight Group Hold
courses.description: Participants in this three-week workshop take steps to develop or refine their analytical thinking skills. They analyze collections of statements to improve their understanding of argumentation and of the nature of validity versus invalidity. They learn various formal rules of reasoning and apply them to assess the strength/effectiveness of arguments. They identify examples of arguments in reports and other media, applying some of the formal rules learned to assess those arguments.

courses.title: AI Readiness
courses.current_step: Provost Continuing Education Module Oversight Group Hold
courses.description: Familiarize yourself with fundamental concepts in data science and learn how leading organizations apply AI for competitive advantage and mission impact. Learn what it takes to execute AI projects successfully to become an AI Champion within your organization.

courses.title: AI Readiness
courses.description: Explores artificial intelligence fundamentals and practical applications for business decision-making. Examines common misconceptions, limitations, and risks associated with AI implementation in organizational contexts. Assesses personal and organizational readiness through diagnostic tools and problem-first frameworks. Introduces structured approaches for translating business challenges into clear instructions for AI tools. Develops skills in evaluating appropriate AI use cases and distinguishing between questions AI can and cannot reliably support. Practices crafting, refining, and testing prompts tied to real business scenarios through peer review and collaborative discussion. Emphasizes the role of human judgment, ethical considerations, and responsible adaptation of AI tools over time. Designed for business leaders seeking practical understanding without technical expertise.

courses.title: Power, Culture and Communication
courses.description: Provides an overview of how systems of power impact culture and communication. Learners will explore how to work within a change-resistant culture. Learners will learn and become aligned around a shared Diversity, Equity, Inclusion, and Justice (DEIJ) vocabulary so that they can effectively communicate as allies, advocates, or "co-conspirators".

courses.title: Foundations of Diversity, Equity and Inclusion
courses.description: This course is designed to provide foundational knowledge of diversity, equity, and inclusion. Learners will examine core DEI concepts, such as power, privilege, oppression, and intersectionality. Learners will participate in reflective discussions and begin the intentional personal exploration of their own feelings and experience with diversity, equity, and inclusion.

courses.title: DEI Change Agency
courses.description: Learners examine their personal positionality, the process of change, and the key characteristics of a change agent in both personal and professional environments. Learners will integrate change management principles into leadership strategies geared toward achieving diversity, equity, and inclusion on a macro and micro level.

courses.title: Facilitating Difficult Dialogues on Diversity, Equity and Inclusion
courses.description: This course focuses on developing foundational skills to facilitate difficult dialogues centered on diversity, equity, and inclusion in organizational settings. Learners explore the purpose and goal of dialogues. They will identify the key principles of facilitation and learn the various approaches to successfully facilitate meaningful and productive discussions on diversity, equity, and inclusion.

courses.title: Connecting Leadership within Diversity Equity and Inclusion Principles
courses.description: This course focuses on the principles of diversity, equity, and inclusion-centered leadership. Learners will examine leadership strategies that promote the creation of an inclusive, organizational culture. Learners will explore ways to develop a shared common purpose to build trust and unity towards diversity, equity, and inclusion.

courses.title: Digital Marketing
courses.description: Introduces practical marketing strategies for small business environments with emphasis on social media, email marketing, customer journey mapping, and performance metrics. Examines customer acquisition and buyer journey development from awareness to conversion. Integrates preparation for industry certifications and highlights actionable approaches tailored to small business contexts and resource constraints.

courses.title: Leader's Challenge Program: Unlocking Potential Through Intrapreneurship
courses.description: Explores collaborative problem-solving in the context of a pressing organizational challenge through the application of intrapreneurship principles. Examines customer discovery processes, development of lean business canvases, and methodologies for testing and iterating initial solutions. Offers learners the opportunity to apply innovative approaches to real-world business challenges.

courses.title: Understanding and Leveraging Large Language Models
courses.description: Provides a hands-on environment for exploring the use of large language models to complete complex tasks. Considers prompt engineering strategies; AI-assisted workflows; and when to use traditional analytics, traditional machine learning, large language models, or AI agents.

courses.title: Ensuring Data Quality & Compliance Through Governance
courses.description: Introduces data governance as a system for ensuring data quality, regulatory compliance, and alignment with organizational goals. Examines how data lineage, metadata management, and risk-mitigation practices build reliable and responsible data ecosystems. Emphasizes practical approaches that support decision-making, protect data integrity, and reduce organizational risk.

courses.title: Global Student Community Navigator
courses.description: Provides hands-on experience in peer advising and student engagement leadership. Students serve as CPS Navigators over a six-month period, supporting prospective, new, and current College of Professional Studies students as they navigate Northeastern University and build connections across campus locations. Through both virtual and on-campus activities, students have an opportunity to facilitate engagement through live events, student panels, livestreams, and one-on-one conversations via The Ambassador Platform (TAP). Explores developing digital content creation skills by producing materials for CPS social media channels and the TAP gallery designed to engage target student audiences. Emphasizes leadership development, communication skills, peer mentorship, and digital engagement strategies in higher education contexts.

courses.title: Evaluating AI Opportunities
courses.current_step: Provost Continuing Education Module Oversight Group
courses.description: Provides a hands-on environment for developing skills to strategically respond to the impact of artificial intelligence in business contexts. Examines methods for evaluating, prioritizing, and implementing AI-driven opportunities to support organizational decision-making and innovation.

courses.title: Identifying AI Opportunities
courses.current_step: Provost Continuing Education Module Oversight Group
courses.description: Introduces frameworks and tools for identifying, assessing, and prioritizing artificial intelligence opportunities within industry and organizational contexts. Examines current AI applications, case studies, and emerging innovations to evaluate strategic value, feasibility, and impact. Explores ethical, regulatory, and governance considerations associated with AI deployment.

courses.title: Essential AI for Leaders
courses.current_step: Provost Continuing Education Module Oversight Group
courses.description: Provides a hands-on environment for learners to focus on industry-specific AI use cases, evaluate industry-relevant AI innovations and current applications to identify the associated ethical and regulatory considerations in deployment, and explore an AI opportunity evaluation framework to strategically deploy AI opportunities.

courses.title: Advanced Prompting Techniques
courses.description: Introduces advanced prompting techniques for the creation of refined prompts. Explores the use of sophisticated features like few-shot learning and project-based environments to maximize the value of AI tools. Identifies optimal use cases for AI use, including when to use AI chats, agents and notebooks / projects.

courses.title: Chemical Safety in the Research Laboratory
courses.current_step: CHEM Graduate Department Curriculum Committee Chair
courses.description: Covers the material needed to complete successfully all the online safety training that is required for our graduate students, best practices for the safe execution of common chemical laboratory procedures, advanced procedures, as well as incidents from the recent literature. Includes discussions of case studies on topics relevant for the safe and effective use of chemicals and other materials in a research laboratory environment. Undergraduates may enroll with permission of the instructor. May be repeated two times.

courses.title: Numerical Strategies and Data Analytics for Chemical Sciences
courses.current_step: Data Entry 6 - NY Scholar MMC Courses
courses.description: Introduces a broad range of numerical methods for solving large-scale, data-driven problems that arise in chemical engineering, chemistry, biochemistry, and other chemical sciences. Offers students an opportunity to obtain a detailed understanding of the derivation, analysis, and use of these numerical methods and learn how these lay the foundations to machine learning techniques. Topics include numerical programming logic, linear and nonlinear algebraic equations, polynomial fitting, numerical calculus, optimization techniques, and large-scale data analysis. Applies to a broad field of chemical and biochemical sciences, where large-scale multivariate data analyses and pattern extraction are key components.

courses.title: Topics in Organic Chemistry
courses.current_approver_emails: a.francois@northeastern.edu; m.giannini@northeastern.edu; c.mattos@northeastern.edu; b.nelson@northeastern.edu; m.rubock@northeastern.edu; x.tan@northeastern.edu
courses.description: Offers various topics within the breadth of organic chemistry. Intended to meet the needs and interests of students. Topics could range from the physical and material aspects of organic chemistry to the biochemical and biomedical aspects of organic chemistry. Undergraduate students who have completed a second semester of organic chemistry with a grade of at least C– may be admitted with permission of instructor. May be repeated once.

courses.title: Engineering for Chemical Sustainability
courses.description: Discusses the rationale and approaches for integrating sustainability into the design of chemical products and processes. Emphasizes technical and design practice and measuring the impacts of products and processes in business, environmental, and social contexts. Introduces students to methods used in green chemical engineering, particularly as applied to reaction engineering, management of the nitrogen and carbon cycles, and decarbonization.

courses.title: Basics of Raman Spectroscopy
courses.description: Introduces the foundational principles, analytical approaches, and practical applications of Raman spectroscopy. Examines how Raman techniques are used to characterize materials, interpret spectral data, and support research and industry practice.

courses.title: Basics of Chromatography-HPLC
courses.description: Introduces the foundational principles, analytical methods, and applications of chromatography with emphasis on high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). Examines how chromatographic techniques are used to separate, identify, and quantify chemical components across research and industry settings.

courses.title: Clean Water Design through Chemical Engineering
courses.current_step: CHME Undergraduate Department Curriculum Committee Chair
courses.description: Discusses clean water sustainability, legal and historical regulations, ethics, and chemical engineering. Examines requirements and approaches to achieving clean water through a historical lens, considering the road to government and institutional regulations in Boston, Oakland, and London. Emphasizes history leading to establishing the Clean Water Act and its impact, with comparison to legal and societal standards in other locations. Addresses case studies including Charles River, Cuyahoga River, and Love Canal; addresses current challenges including PFAS and desalinated water supplies. Discusses chemical engineering approaches enabling clean water (filtration, reverse osmosis, fluid mechanics), including how associated regulations are established. Includes design, build, and analysis of pilot-scale water treatment systems. Serves as an overall introduction to chemical engineering concepts, grounded in societal needs and government and industrial standards.

courses.title: Engineering Thermodynamics Abroad
courses.description: Covers the first and second laws of thermodynamics and their application to batch and flow systems, heat effects in chemicals, and physical properties/real fluids. Applies basic principles and mathematical relations to the analysis and solution of engineering problems. Taught abroad.

courses.title: Numerical Strategies and Data Analytics for Chemical Sciences
courses.current_step: Data Entry 6 - NY Scholar MMC Courses
courses.description: Introduces a broad range of numerical methods for solving large-scale, data-driven problems that arise in chemical engineering, chemistry, biochemistry, and other chemical sciences. Offers students an opportunity to obtain a detailed understanding of the derivation, analysis, and use of these numerical methods and learn how these lay the foundations to machine learning techniques. Topics include numerical programming logic, linear and nonlinear algebraic equations, polynomial fitting, numerical calculus, optimization techniques, and large-scale data analysis. Applies to a broad field of chemical and biochemical sciences, where large-scale multivariate data analyses and pattern extraction are key components.

courses.title: Engineering for Chemical Sustainability
courses.description: Discusses the rationale and approaches for integrating sustainability into the design of chemical products and processes. Emphasizes technical and design practice and measuring the impacts of products and processes in business, environmental, and social contexts. Introduces students to methods used in green chemical engineering, particularly as applied to reaction engineering, management of the nitrogen and carbon cycles, and decarbonization.

courses.title: Statics and Solid Mechanics
courses.current_step: CIVE Undergraduate Department Curriculum Committee Chair
courses.description: Connects fundamentals of Newtonian physics to the stresses and deformations in solids. Introduces properties of areas and volumes (centroidal axes, moments of inertia); equilibrium of particles and rigid bodies in two and three dimensions; analysis of internal forces in trusses and simple frames; shear and moment diagrams in beams; computation of stresses induced by axial force, moment, shear, and torque; and mechanical properties of materials.

courses.title: Climate and Atmospheric Change
courses.current_step: Data Entry 6 - NY Scholar MMC Courses
courses.description: Offers an in-depth view of the processes that drive change in Earth's climate system. Examines the modern climate system and how and why climate changes through time. Introduces the tools used to explore past climates and changes, and explores the long-term and short-term controls on the climate system. Also introduces the application of climate models to develop future climate projections. Offers students an opportunity to obtain hands-on experience analyzing and interpreting climate data and model output.

courses.title: Communication Studies at Northeastern
courses.description: Designed to provide a unique opportunity to engage faculty, professional staff, and peer mentors in small group discussions. Introduces students to the College of Arts, Media and Design. Offers students an opportunity to learn about the communication studies major and to explore the different areas of emphasis offered by the department. As part of the course, students are expected to prepare a detailed plan of study and are introduced to the co-op program and meet their academic co-op advisor.

courses.title: Communicating Science, Technology, and the Environment
courses.description: Covers research and analysis of case studies in topics related to science, technology, and the environment, and culminates in design of a strategic communication plan. Case studies will include a range of topics, such as conflicts over siting data centers; engaging local publics on issues like climate change; and debates over ethics and governance of emerging technologies like artificial intelligence. Builds on previous coursework in science and environmental communication to interrogate and apply theory and practical principles within communication about complex scientific and environmental issues. Draws on multidisciplinary areas of theory, research, and practice to provide historical, cultural, and political contexts for each topic; assess communication barriers and sources of conflict; and apply theory and empirical evidence to design solutions.

courses.title: Professional Communication: Writing, Speaking, and Collaboration
courses.description: Offers students an opportunity to increase their professional communication skills through intensive verbal practice and technical writing application. Students work together in groups and individually to practice verbal and written communication that can increase their English competency and comfort level for work in the United States. This course does not count toward graduation requirements.

courses.title: Personal and Career Development
courses.description: Offers students an opportunity to use co-op experience along with this course to clarify their vision of a successful professional and personal future and identify goals to create that vision; identify strengths, weaknesses, and communication and conflict-management preferences; design a career action plan; and develop and practice articulating professional goals, personal brand, and knowledge and experience gained from co-op. Encourages students to engage in a combination of introspection, critical reflection on experiences in the workplace, and with online collaborative learning and group behavior; learn to identify and analyze career and personal development opportunities in the external environment; and practice communication, relationship-building, conflict management, and leadership skills. This companion course to an internship or co-op requires permission of the CPS Office of Cooperative Education.

courses.title: Internship
courses.description: Enables students to engage in an internship to gain practical experience relating to their field of study. May be repeated twice.

courses.title: Innovation for Impact: the Carlson-Polizzotto Method Fundamentals of Value Creation
courses.current_step: Provost Continuing Education Module Oversight Group Hold
courses.description: TheInnovation for Impact value creation series is divided into three courses, with concepts building upon each other as you move through the material. You will engage in value creation forums along the way, which are essential for iterating, reframing, and refining your value proposition and action plan. During this first course, you will explore the fundamental concepts of value creation that include value proposition best practices and the NABC framework. You will apply key value creation concepts while engaging with case studies that will further shape how to develop your plan; and you will practice active listening while engaging with course peers.

courses.title: Ascend for Senior Leaders
courses.description: Explores leadership practices for senior-level managers, emphasizing self-leadership, strategic communication across levels of an organization, navigating high-stakes conversations, and guiding agile, high-performing teams.

courses.title: Business Management Essentials - Marketing and Communications
courses.description: Introduces approaches for effective marketing and communications within a business context. Examines how managers build clarity, empathy, and credibility with key audiences. Explores strategies for aligning messaging with organizational goals and for strengthening professional presence. Offers opportunities to apply concepts to real-world scenarios.

courses.title: Navigating Leadership
courses.description: Explores organizational purpose, performance measurement, and accountability. Examines approaches for motivating teams, collaborating across functions, and aligning departmental work with enterprise-level goals.

courses.title: Business Management Essentials - Operations Management
courses.description: Introduces core concepts in operations management for small and medium-sized business environments. Examines approaches to designing efficient workflows, managing capacity and resources, strengthening quality systems, and using data to inform operational decisions. Explores frameworks for process analysis, inventory considerations, scheduling, and performance measurement to support effective and scalable operations.

courses.title: Business Management Essentials - Finance
courses.description: Introduces foundational financial management concepts for small and medium-sized businesses. Examines approaches to interpreting financial statements, analyzing costs, assessing cash flow needs, and developing budgets that support operational goals. Explores frameworks for evaluating pricing decisions, understanding working capital considerations, and using financial metrics to inform strategic choices.

courses.title: Embark Leadership Program
courses.current_step: Provost Continuing Education Module Oversight Group
courses.description: Introduces foundational leadership principles and practices to support emerging and high-potential leaders. Explores self-awareness, team dynamics, and organizational collaboration in the context of strategic execution and change. Examines approaches to integrating new technologies within business operations and introduces financial levers that inform decision-making, organizational performance, and customer value creation.

courses.title: Vital Innovation and Transformation for Agile Leadership: Preparing Healthcare Leaders for the Age of AI and Accelerated Change
