courses.description: Provides learners with the skills to effectively lead healthcare organizations in the unpredictable and rapidly changing technological, cultural and economic landscape. Learners should expect to gain insight into managing themselves, and others in the age of AI, the business and financial sustainability of healthcare, supply chain and workforce challenges in today’s economy and innovation and transformation needed to prepare for the future. Gives learners tools to address challenges faced by healthcare institutions in today's geopolitical realities.

courses.title: Catalyst: The Art of Leading
courses.description: Explores the skills that cross-functional senior leaders need to effectively lead high performing teams and large, complex organizations in today's rapidly changing market. Provides opportunities to practice the power skills needed to influence, coach, and create powerful strategic narratives, and the tactical skills to execute growth plans and manage through disruption.

courses.title: Foundations of Management for Emerging Leaders in Higher Education
courses.current_step: Provost Continuing Education Module Oversight Group
courses.description: Offers learners an opportunity to develop an understanding of interpersonal dynamics and teambuilding, while reflecting on their own leadership practices, professional presence, and communication skills. Provides practical instruction on digital tools and use of AI, and gives participants hands-on experience developing skills to implement new technology to increase efficiency and maximize team productivity. Guides learners to amplify their business acumen, effectiveness, and impact as they grow as leaders within the higher education context.

courses.title: Building Software with Agentic AI
courses.description: Explores leveraging AI agents as collaborative partners throughout the software development lifecycle—from initial concept to deployed product. Bridges the gap between AI capabilities and practical software delivery. Offers opportunities to orchestrate AI agents for scoping requirements, architecting solutions, generating code, and shipping working applications. Emphasizes strategic integration of AI tools into development workflows.

courses.title: Programming Design Concepts
courses.current_step: Provost Continuing Education Module Oversight Group Hold
courses.description: Introduces programming design concepts. Explores how to design small to medium size computer programs by taking a problem statement, breaking it into sub-problems, solving the sub-problems, and then combining the sub-problem solutions together into a complete program. Explores and provides opportunities to apply concepts such as abstraction, encapsulation, top-down design, unit testing, documentation, and good programming practices. Offers learners the opportunity to use Python programming language and associated tools to write and test code to solve small to medium-scale problems.

courses.title: Object-Oriented Design Concepts
courses.current_step: Provost Continuing Education Module Oversight Group Hold
courses.description: An introduction to object-oriented programming concepts. Working at their own pace, students will learn how to build classes, create parent and child classes, override methods, create static class variables, build accessor methods, create objects, and use objects to call methods or access fields. Students will learn and practice concepts such as encapsulation, polymorphism, inheritance, basic linear data structures, recursion, and sorting. Students will use the Python programming language and associated tools to write and test code to solve small to medium-scale problems using object-oriented design principles.

courses.title: Computer Vision and Machine Learning
courses.current_step: Provost Continuing Education Module Oversight Group Hold
courses.description: An in-depth study of how computers can learn to perceive the world using cameras and other sensors. Topics will include unsupervised learning methods, clustering, principal components analysis, and deep network architectures for object detection, classification, and localization. Participants will implement a variety of machine learning techniques, including deep convolutional networks, and apply them to images and other sensor data to solve business relevant problems. Participants will also review, present, and discuss technical papers on the core topics and deep network architectures.

courses.title: Computational Methods for Proteomics and Mass Spectrometry Based Data Interpretation
courses.description: Introduces computational and statistical approaches for analyzing and interpreting mass spectrometry data. Examines quantitative proteomics, mass spectrometry imaging, and causal inference methods to support rigorous data-driven research. Develops conceptual understanding and practical problem-solving skills for working with complex biological datasets. Emphasizes methodological rigor, analytical reasoning, and the application of computational tools to scientific inquiry and research design.

courses.title: Python Development for AI Applications
courses.description: Presents an in-depth exploration of programming paradigms, mathematical foundations, and computational techniques essential for scientific computing and data-driven applications. Integrates linear algebra, Python programming principles, algorithmic design, and data science methodologies to offer participants an opportunity to develop the technical expertise necessary for handling complex numerical computations and large-scale data processing. Strongly emphasizes Pythonic programming, object-oriented and functional programming, data preprocessing, visualization, and numerical stability, with the aim of developing the skills needed to write clean, efficient, and maintainable code for scientific and engineering applications.

courses.title: Data Structures and Analysis
courses.description: Introduces data structures and analysis. Investigates abstract data types, recursion, algorithms for searching and sorting, and basic algorithm analysis. Covers abstract data types (ADTs)including arrays, linked lists, stacks, queues, priority queues, trees, and hash tables. Demonstrates ADT operations such as insert, remove, and find. Examines the trade-offs associated with ADT choices for a given application and the trade-offs associated with array-based implementations compared to linked implementations. Offers students an opportunity to apply core concepts by implementing their own data structures and developing applications that use them.

courses.title: Empirical Research Methods
courses.description: Evaluates and conducts empirical research, focusing on students’ use of empirical methods to study the effectiveness and organizational/social impact of information systems and technologies. Empirical research involves a number of broad steps including identifying problems; developing specific hypotheses; collecting data relevant to the hypotheses; analyzing the data; and considering alternative explanations for the empirical findings. Some of the most commonly used research techniques, such as surveys, experiments, and ethnographic methods, are discussed. Additional topics include the ethics of data collection and experimentation in behavioral science. Although the course focuses primarily on the relationship between formulating research questions and implementing the appropriate methods to answer them, students can expect to apply the statistical techniques learned in the course prerequisites.

courses.title: Data Visualization with Web Technologies
courses.description: Introduces techniques and web technologies for visually communicating insights derived from data. Focuses on developing apps using open-source libraries to explore and actively manipulate data through interactive data visualizations. Critical discussion of case studies motivates experiments with a variety of technologies and techniques. Includes interactive charts, web maps, and algorithm visualization and emphasizes practical, real-world solutions.

courses.title: Engaging with Industry Partners for Rising Professionals
courses.current_approver_emails: m.ormerod@northeastern.edu; k.melchin@northeastern.edu; b.hescott@northeastern.edu; a.jamieson@northeastern.edu; a.shesh@northeastern.edu; a.ahmed@northeastern.edu; c.presby@northeastern.edu
courses.description: Offers students an enhanced applied project setting in which to apply their curricular learning. Working with a partner sponsor, students refine an applied research topic, perform research, develop recommendations that are shared with the partner sponsor, and create a plan for implementing their recommendations. Curriculum supports students as they develop key business communication skills, project and client management skills, and frameworks for business analysis. Offers students an opportunity to learn from sponsor feedback, review lessons learned, and incorporate suggestions to improve and further hone their career development and professional plan. Career development opportunities through skill-building workshops, panels, and interview preparation are available. Partner-student interactions, including a culminating project presentation, allow partners to assess student potential for co-op, internship, or other employment opportunities with the partner. May be repeated two times.

courses.title: Machine Learning
courses.description: Provides a broad look at a variety of techniques used in machine learning and data mining, and also examines issues associated with their use. Topics include algorithms for supervised learning including decision tree induction, artificial neural networks, instance-based learning, probabilistic methods, and support vector machines; unsupervised learning; and reinforcement learning. Also covers computational learning theory and other methods for analyzing and measuring the performance of learning algorithms. Course work includes a programming term project.

courses.title: Introduction to Theory and Practical Applications of AI Generative Modeling
courses.description: Focuses on both theory and applications, offering students an opportunity to learn the quantitative models and metrics that drive the success of popular generative AI applications such as recurrent neural networks, long short-term memory model, attention mechanism, transformer models, convolutional neural networks, generative adversarial networks, reinforcement learning, and deep Q-learning.

courses.title: Introduction to AI Agent Infrastructure
courses.description: Covers core aspects of distributed systems that make their design and implementation more challenging than sequential software running on a single machine. Introduces the Akka toolkit with emphasis on the actor-based programming model, highlighting its utility for building scalable, resilient, and concurrent applications. Explores key Akka modules that support message-driven development and distributed computing. Emphasizes practical techniques for building, validating, and maintaining robust distributed applications in modern software environment.

courses.title: Introduction to Agentic AI
courses.description: Explores practical development of autonomous AI agents through classical and modern techniques. Covers foundational methods including rule-based logic, reactive and reflexive behaviors, and scripted decision trees, alongside contemporary approaches using Large Language Models (LLMs). Emphasizes comparative analysis of agent architectures with and without LLM integration, highlighting trade-offs in adaptability, interpretability, and performance. Incorporates real-world industry-inspired scenarios to guide the design and implementation of practical, context-aware agents capable of operating in dynamic environments.

courses.title: Graph Machine Learning
courses.description: Introduces graph machine learning (GML) techniques for modeling relational data in social networks, molecular biology, and knowledge graphs. Emphasizes unique challenges of graph-structured data, including non-Euclidean geometry and topological dependencies. Covers foundational and advanced methods such as Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), with applications in node classification, link prediction, and graph generation. Explores model properties like permutation invariance, message passing, and scalability, and examines enabling generalizable, structure-aware learning. Highlights practical implementations and use cases in areas including drug discovery, recommendation systems, and cybersecurity.

courses.title: Deep Reinforcement Learning Agents
courses.description: Provides a comprehensive introduction to deep reinforcement learning (DRL), focusing on intelligent agent development capable of sequential decision-making in complex, high-dimensional environments. Explores core concepts including Markov decision processes, value-based and policy-based methods, actor-critic architectures, and model-based reinforcement learning. Emphasizes practical implementation using deep neural networks to approximate value functions and policies, as well as strategies for improving sample efficiency, stability, and safety during training. Applies DRL techniques to real-world challenges such as robotics control, supply chain management, and dynamic resource allocation. Covers topics in transfer learning, meta-learning, and safe exploration to prepare students for advanced applications and research in autonomous systems.

courses.title: Causal AI
courses.description: Introduces the theory and practice of Causal AI, focusing on how causal reasoning enhances the robustness, interpretability, and fairness of machine learning systems. Covers fundamentals of causal inference, including causal graphs, structural causal models, and do-calculus. Presents techniques for identifying and estimating causal effects, handling confounding, and designing interventions. Examines practical applications in healthcare, economics, and policy-making. Covers the skills to design, implement, and evaluate causal models for real-world decision-making scenarios.

courses.title: Post-Training of Large Language Models
courses.description: Focuses on post-training methodologies for large language models. Emphasizes their necessity in overcoming the inherent limitations of pretrained architectures in logical reasoning, ethical alignment, and domain specialization. Analyzes the design and implementation of post-trained language models, including large reasoning models. Examines five core paradigms: supervised and reinforcement-based fine-tuning for task adaptation; alignment via preference modeling and RLHF; reasoning enhancement through structured prompting and reward optimization; efficiency techniques for scaling under computational constraints; and multimodal integration for extending functionality. Introduces a formal taxonomy of PoLM approaches, connecting algorithmic techniques to performance metrics and dataset properties. Discusses how post-training frameworks enable LLMs to achieve higher fidelity, interpretability, and generalization in complex, high-stakes domains.

courses.title: Special Topics in AI System Design and Engineering
courses.description: Offers various topics related to principles and methods behind designing and engineering large-scale AI systems. Possible topics include distributed architectures, data pipelines, and modular AI components that enable scalability, reliability, and ethical operation of intelligent systems. Specific topics vary by semester. May be repeated up to three times for a maximum of 16 semester hours.

courses.title: Special Topics in Agent Learning Models
courses.description: Presents selected topics in learning paradigms in autonomous agents, including reinforcement learning, multi-agent coordination, adaptive decision-making, and emergent behaviors. Specific topics vary by semester. May be repeated up to three times for a maximum of 16 semester hours.

courses.title: Special Topics in Agent Infrastructures
courses.description: Explores specialized topics related to the infrastructures and frameworks that support intelligent agents, including distributed simulation environments, multi-agent communication protocols, orchestration layers, and agent-based modeling platforms. Specific topics vary by semester. May be repeated up to three times for a maximum of 16 semester hours.

courses.title: Special Topics in AI and Inferencing Systems
courses.description: Offers various topics in advanced inferencing architectures and reasoning systems in artificial intelligence, including probabilistic reasoning, knowledge graphs, neural-symbolic integration, and large-scale inference engines. Specific topics vary by semester. May be repeated up to three times for a maximum of 16 semester hours.

courses.title: Data Warehousing and Integration
courses.description: Covers various topics in data engineering in support of decision support systems, data analytics, data mining, machine learning, and artificial intelligence. Studies on-premises data warehouse architecture, dimensional modeling of data warehouses, ETL integration from source systems to data warehouse, OLAP systems, and the evolving world of data quality and data governance. Offers students an opportunity to design, develop, and maintain cloud-based data pipelines. Uses both on-premises and cloud-based platforms to illustrate and implement data engineering techniques using operational and analytical data warehouses.

courses.title: Natural Language Processing for Interdisciplinary Applications
courses.description: Offers an in-depth exploration of NLP techniques, their underlying principles, and their applications in engineering. Focuses on the practical implementation of NLP methods such as word embeddings, neural networks, attention mechanisms, and advanced deep learning models to solve real-world engineering problems.

courses.title: Deep Learning for AI
courses.description: Offers an in-depth exploration of modern deep learning architectures and methodologies that drive today's AI systems. Covers both foundational and advanced models with theoretical understanding and practical skills through programming assignments and projects. Covers convolutional and recurrent networks, generative models, transformers, diffusion models, multimodal learning, and emerging approaches such as world models. Emphasizes both mathematical rigor and real-world applications across computer vision, natural language processing, and generative AI.

courses.title: Deep Generative Methods
courses.description: Examines theoretical foundations of generative AI across natural language processing and computer vision. Covers deep learning architectures including autoencoders, GANs, diffusion models, and transformers. Investigates applications such as text generation, image synthesis, and retrieval-augmented generation. Reinforces concepts through a structured lab sequence that supports practical understanding and ethical considerations in AI development.

courses.title: Projects
courses.description: Provides students with the opportunity to create individual choreographic works rehearsed by students and faculty for the fall production, which includes the student choreography workshop and the mainstage performances. Student choreographers are mentored by faculty throughout the semester. Auditions and casting culminate in bi-weekly rehearsals that afford experimentation, creative practices and realization of both technical and artistic voices in performance. All aspects of producing a dance concert are addressed including publicity, costuming, lighting, and scene design. Both the Great Hall and the Theresa Lang Theatre serve as venues. May be repeated up to three times.

courses.title: Experiential Anatomy
courses.description: Offers students an opportunity to develop a practical, functional, and theoretical understanding of basic anatomical terms and principles. Applies current dance research in kinesiology and physiology to concepts of movement analysis, alignment assessment, and performance enhancement. Introduces a kinesthetic exploration of skeletal joint mechanics and the muscles that produce motion of those joints. Students apply movement analysis to the evaluation of dance technique of themselves and others.

courses.title: Dance Composition 1
courses.description: Introduces students to the nature of composition through a variety of disciplines including music, visual art, poetry, and in particular dance. Examines diverse choreographic styles developed in response to cultural, philosophical, and artistic influences as a foundation for thinking critically about dance. Classes are structured around compositional exercises, movement analysis of choreographic homework assignments, readings, and viewing seminal works of art on video or in museums. Seeks to offer a forum through which students can deeply engage with movement creation, develop their own artistic voices ,and investigate new ways of thinking about form through the lens of choreographic inquiry.

courses.title: Dance and Cultural Histories
courses.description: Introduces major concepts, approaches, and issues in the study of dance as a cultural, historical, and artistic practice. Incorporates a critique of Western dance history, the dance canon, mastery, and the focus on aesthetic representation as benchmarks of success in dance. Examines important developments in dance in a global context, as we critically reflect on our assumptions on what knowledge in dance is and how it is structured. As such, we question the supremacy of Western dance forms as the standard for dance training, production, and performance and examine who has the right to speak about dance, who decides on ways to move, and consider who is excluded from these movements. Seeks to understand how dance practices are bodily enactments of specific historical, cultural, and political developments and to critically investigate different approaches to dance and the writing of its history.

courses.title: Critical Approaches to Dance
courses.description: Explores what dance is, how we study it, and what dance can do in society. Analyzes critical approaches in the field of dance studies and key issues raised by dance. Such analysis enables us to situate specific dance examples and the creation and interpretation of dance in relation to culture in a global context. Offers students an opportunity to understand dance as a social structure and how dance and culture develop out of shared aesthetics as well as political principles. This allows analysis of agency in dance and choreography and addresses their role toward the creation of a socially just, environmentally conscious, and decolonized society. Taught in the studio and values and approaches the moved and choreographed the same as the written and spoken.

courses.title: American Sign Language at Northeastern
courses.description: Introduces liberal arts in general, campus and program resources, and career options for students of American Sign Language (ASL). Offers students an opportunity to develop the academic and interpersonal skills necessary to become a successful university student (analytical ability, and critical thinking). Provides grounding in culturally appropriate behaviors and values of Deaf communities and introduces students to local Deaf community organizations, and the activities of the ASL & Interpreting Education Program (ASLIE).

courses.title: Research Methods for Global User Experiences
Search Engine Optimization and Brand Strategy
Introduction to Customer Experience
courses.description: Introduces students to the fundamental principles and components of delivering value to customers, and how to keep them continuously engaged through interactions in a variety of channels with a company’s product portfolio. Covers the importance of satisfaction and personal identification with brand, experiential marketing, product and service throughout the customer journey.

courses.title: Managing Digital Media Projects
courses.description: Introduces and integrates the project management life cycle into the development of technology-based media products. Beginning with project initiation, explores the principles and tools needed to successfully plan and manage a creative, technology-driven project. Major topics include assembling a team; strategic leadership decisions (roles, balancing technical and creative goals, cross-functional collaboration); tracking assets; project communication and documentation; deadlines and client expectations; and balancing continuous improvement and rapid prototyping against the need to manage the scope of work.

courses.title: Professional Practicum Capstone
courses.description: Offers students an opportunity to expand skills in real-world machine learning, artificial Intelligence, and data science applications and team collaboration within an experiential learning structure. Involves students in industry-specific projects designed to integrate into an industry partner’s intellectual property portfolio. Projects focus on machine learning and artificial intelligence and also include written components that align with the partner’s standards and reference relevant prior work. Students engage in a structured process of milestones and feedback cycles with peers; instructors; and, as appropriate, industry partners to refine project outcomes.

courses.title: Mathematics for Economics
courses.current_step: ECON Undergraduate Department Curriculum Committee Chair
courses.description: Develops an understanding of the uses of mathematics in the study of economics, with a particular emphasis on single-variate and bivariate calculus and optimization. After a brief review of algebra and equations, it covers the mathematical concepts of functions, single- and bivariate calculus (differentiation and some integration), unconstrained and constrained optimization, elasticity, implicit differentiation, comparative statics, value functions, and other concepts. Economic applications are provided as each of these mathematical concepts are discussed.

courses.title: Data Analysis Tools for Economists
courses.description: Provides students with an overview of popular software packages used by economists when carrying out data analysis. The first part of the course offers an overview of the software Excel and its capabilities with regards to data collection, importing, cleaning, merging, exploration, summarization, and visualization. The second part of the course covers similar types of data analysis with at least two of the following three programming software: R, Python, and Stata. In this second stage, students utilize said software to write their own code for data analysis. Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools are also introduced as a complement to each of these software tools and techniques.

courses.title: Macroeconomic Theory
courses.description: Presents several theoretical approaches to the study of macroeconomic dynamics in both the short and long run. Emphasizes the use of rigorous mathematical tools, such as calculus, to examine the major determinants of fluctuations in employment and price level, as well as the rate of economic growth. Explores theoretical models to evaluate the operation and impact of various macroeconomic policy tools. Discusses how macroeconomic performance relates to issues of social justice.

courses.title: Microeconomic Theory
courses.description: Examines the behavior of consumers and firms under several different market structures, including monopoly, oligopoly, monopolistic competition, and perfect competition. Covers sources of market failure, such as public goods, externalities, and information asymmetry. Presents the key analytical tools economists apply to evaluate the welfare and distributional impacts of policies designed to improve market outcomes. Relies on calculus-based methods, offering an opportunity for students to extend their knowledge of microeconomics.

courses.title: Statistics for Economists
courses.description: Presents statistical techniques used to analyze data in order to address issues related to economics and other social sciences. Explores different types of datasets, sampling, and data collection techniques. Discusses how to obtain sociodemographic data for different subpopulations based on gender, race, class, ethnicity, and other stratifications. Uses data visualization techniques to depict economic phenomena. Offers students an opportunity to compute and interpret summary statistics, conduct confidence interval estimation, hypothesis testing, and simple regression analysis. Computer applications are an integral part of the course.

courses.title: Applied Econometrics
courses.description: Introduces the techniques of regression analysis useful to address relevant questions in economics and related subjects. Covers the foundations of multivariate regression analysis, including potential issues such as heteroskedasticity, multicollinearity, omitted variable bias, and model specification. Additional topics may include instrumental variables, panel data, discrete choice models, and time-series analysis. Utilizes regression software (e.g., Stata, R, Python) throughout the course to analyze real-world data. Writing an applied econometrics paper is an integral part of the course.

courses.title: Development Economics
courses.current_step: Data Entry 6 - NY Scholar MMC Courses
courses.description: Explores social and economic development around the world. Topics include income, poverty, inequality, human development, geography, growth, impact evaluation, health, education, financial markets, trade, and gender inequality. Analyzes four key elements of economic development: income, poverty, inequality, and human development. Offers students an opportunity to understand the determinants of economic growth. Focuses on major policy issues concerning health, education, credit, savings, gender differences, and globalization. Studies which interventions worked and which did not. Exposes students to readings and perspectives from several academic disciplines. Emphasizes one unifying methodological theme: the usefulness of empirical economic tools in assessing the arguments presented in debates about development.

courses.title: Health Economics and Healthcare Policy
courses.description: Studies functional skills economists use in health policy analysis, how to apply economic models, and the tools of data and statistical analysis, with the goal of answering health policy questions. Topics include individual health decisions, health insurance coverage and access to care, the behavior of hospitals and health insurers, the Medicare and Medicaid programs, prescription drug prices, innovation/R&D in the pharmaceutical sector, and topics in public health. Offers students an opportunity to develop the knowledge and tools required to understand, discuss, and provide informed perspective on national policy debates, such as the Affordable Care Act, rising healthcare prices and lack of affordability, the benefits and drawbacks of a national single-payer health insurance program, and public health topics such as rising mortality and prescription opioid and heroin abuse.

courses.title: Behavioral Economics
courses.description: Questions assumptions made in standard microeconomic utility-driven models, using insights from psychology and other social sciences. Examines behavior that departs from these assumptions and tests theories with data. Incorporates empirical evidence from a wide range of fields, including development economics, health economics, labor economics, industrial organization, and finance. Topics include deviations from standard models in terms of preferences (present bias, reference dependence, and social preferences); beliefs (projection bias); and decision making (cognition, attention, and framing); as well as market and policy reactions to such deviations.

courses.title: Economics of Conflict and Peace
courses.description: Applies economic analysis to the choice between violent conflict and peaceful settlement in dispute resolution at the macrolevel (for example, interstate war, terrorism, genocide) and microlevel (for example, gang warfare, human trafficking, cybercrime). Offers students an opportunity to build a toolkit of models and methods to study this choice such as rational choice theory, game theory, behavioral economics, identity economics, and network economics. Reviews empirical research into the sources of conflict and risk factors for war.

courses.title: Financial Economics
courses.description: Introduces students to the theory of investments, including the principles of risk and return, the theory of portfolio selection, asset pricing models such as the capital asset pricing model (CAPM) and arbitrage pricing theory (APT), valuation of stocks, bond pricing and the term structure of interest rates, and options (what they are and how to use them). Geared toward nonbusiness majors who are interested in a rigorous course in finance.

courses.title: Information Economics and Game Theory
courses.description: Offers an advanced course on the economics of information, including moral hazard and adverse selection; game theory; and mechanism design. Formally considers alternative solution concepts, such as Nash equilibrium and rationalizability for simultaneous move and sequential move games under complete information about payoffs and preferences, as well as solution concepts, such as Bayesian-Nash equilibrium to analyze selection, screening, and incentives in games of incomplete or asymmetric information. Covers optimal incentives or mechanism design, including the optimal design of contracts, auctions, and other mechanisms. Prior exposure to game theory recommended.

courses.title: Senior Economics Seminar
courses.description: Incorporates aspects of real-world and academic experiences of students into an analytical context, enabling students to demonstrate their ability to apply economic concepts, methodology, and data to economic issues and problems of personal and philosophical significance.

courses.title: Internship
courses.current_step: ECON Undergraduate Department Curriculum Committee Chair
courses.description: Offers students an opportunity for internship work.

courses.title: MAT Pre-Gateway Preparation
courses.description: Introduces students to policies and administrative processes required for placement in pre-practicum field experiences. Ensures teacher candidates complete state-mandated requirements signaling their readiness and eligibility for placement in a Massachusetts public school environment in conjunction with academic course requirements leading toward endorsement for licensure by the MAT program.

courses.title: MAT Gateway 1
courses.description: Guides students through state-mandated requirements for supervised teaching pre-practicum in a Massachusetts public school, including information sessions, administrative tasks, and other state-mandated requirements for field placement.

courses.title: MAT Gateway 2
courses.description: Guides students through mandatory information sessions, orientations, and administrative tasks that support the professional and subject-matter knowledge development needed for teaching practicum. Provides students with opportunities to engage with key stakeholders, such as the MAT University Supervisor, Host Teacher, and School Administrator in the Massachusetts public school in which the student completes a pre-practicum field experience.

courses.title: MAT Student Teaching Summative Assessments and Exit Process
courses.current_step: Provost Continuing Education Module Oversight Group Hold
courses.description: Facilitates documentation of completed Field Office tasks and the required exit interview with the MAT faculty lead. Completed in conjunction with remaining academic coursework and/or other requirements for licensure endorsement by the Northeastern University Field Office.

courses.title: Digital Learning Tools and Technologies
courses.description: Explores and experiments with emerging tools and technologies shaping and enhancing the future of learning. Evaluates the value-add of tools and technologies for targeted scenarios. Investigates the impact of copyright and licensing on resources. Offers students an opportunity to engage in research and collaborative projects to apply theoretical knowledge;to reflect on professional practice; and to develop strategies for continuous improvement, applying knowledge to individual contexts.

courses.title: Foundations of Literacy Development and Instruction
Introduction to Qualitative Research Methods
courses.current_approver_emails: a.aamer@northeastern.edu; m.boudreault@northeastern.edu; c.moffatt@northeastern.edu; m.saxena@northeastern.edu; c.bolick@northeastern.edu; k.thierer@northeastern.edu
courses.description: Examines the theory and philosophy behind qualitative, quantitative, and mixed-methods approaches to action research. Additional course topics include understanding research paradigms, translating theory to practice, and exploring the roles of stakeholders. Offers learners an opportunity to gain skills in collecting data, creating research questions, and exploring ethical considerations of conducting research.

courses.title: Global and Historical Perspectives on Higher Education
Learning and AI: Theory, Ethics, Implementation
courses.description: Examines artificial intelligence in learning contexts through three phases: AI fundamentals (machine learning, prompt engineering, AI literacy); ethical considerations (bias, privacy, responsible use);and practical implementation (policies, assessment, change management). Offers students an opportunity to complete hands-on projects including creating AI literacy modules, developing ethical frameworks, and designing implementation plans. Designed for educators and learning professionals to responsibly integrate AI into educational environments.

courses.title: AI in Educational Research and Practice: Critical Inquiry and Applied Practice
courses.description: Examines artificial intelligence applications in educational contexts. Through empirical research of current, use-inspired projects, incorporates engagement with a variety of AI tools and methodologies and critical analysis of AI to enhance learning and research. Encompasses a comprehensive review of AI's role in pedagogy, learning systems design, and educational research. Includes original research on AI's efficacy in enhancing student engagement and learning outcomes including substantive literature review, data collection, and analysis. Offers doctoral candidates an opportunity to advance the integration of AI in educational theory and practice.

courses.title: Thesis Findings and Discussion
courses.description: Supports the processes associated with writing the results and discussion chapters of the thesis. Highlights the scholar-practitioner aspect of the program’s mission, requiring that students think carefully about the practical implications of their work and how they plan to communicate or disseminate those implications to an authentic audience and engage relevant stakeholders in a relevant application of their findings. Successful completion is determined by a student’s defense of the final thesis work that is approved by their thesis committee.

courses.title: Dissertation 1
courses.description: Offers students an opportunity for continued dissertation work conducted under the supervision of their faculty chair towards the completion of the doctoral degree.

courses.title: Dissertation 4
courses.description: Offers students an opportunity for continued dissertation work conducted under the supervision of their faculty chair toward the completion of the doctoral degree.

courses.title: Dissertation Continuation
courses.description: Offers students an opportunity for continued dissertation work conducted under the supervision of their faculty chair toward the completion of the doctoral degree. May be repeated up to three times.

courses.title: Leadership by Design: Creating Conditions for Adult Learning
courses.description: Develops the leadership capacity of established and emerging leaders in both the educational and non-profit sectors, with an emphasis on design and facilitation of powerful adult learning spaces that center the experience of youth. Supports leaders to disrupt unjust practices, cultivate curiosity, and drive continuous improvement across organizational systems. Offers a reflective, robust community of practice including asynchronous guided writing prompts and quarterly synchronous learning experiences that develop leader identity and skill while connecting to deeper purpose, vision for equity, and core values.

courses.title: Experiential Business Decision Making
courses.description: Offers guided instruction about problem identification, problem-solving skills, and working as a team to meet the needs of internal and external stakeholders. The instructor and selected industry experts work with student teams through lectures, question-and-answer sessions, and team feedback sessions to offer real-world feedback. Offers students an opportunity to leverage skills and thinking through an immersive experience; develop observations and reflections of complex situations or challenges; frame the challenges, drawing on concepts, observed patterns, and experiences; synthesize insights into a creative solution; and apply the learnings to new situations.

courses.title: Persuasive Sales, Personal and Professional
courses.description: Introduces selling and the industry of B2B professional selling. Focuses on sales skills that span any function. Covers selling roles and mastery of key selling and presenting skills. Emphasizes heightening persuasive selling skills needed personally and professionally to achieve success. Examines the use of negotiation to achieve the best possible outcomes. Engages in hands-on exercises and real-world simulations to hone sales skills across a range of different scenarios.

courses.title: Managing Operational Disruption in Healthcare
courses.description: Studies the current and ever-changing healthcare environment and potential disruptions to healthcare organizations. Covers skills needed for problem identification, problem solving, and operational execution within the organization. Offers students an opportunity to leverage skills and thinking through a highly experiential, immersive experience; develop observations and reflections of complex situations or challenges; frame the challenges, drawing on concepts, observed patterns, and experiences; synthesize insights into a creative solution; and apply the learnings to new situations.

courses.title: Brand Development and Advertising Application
courses.description: Explores the landscape of brand building amid ever-evolving marketing communication platforms. Utilizes inquiry-based research practices and experiential study culminating in a project to delve into the art of brand development and advertising. Offers students an opportunity to apply key concepts such as positioning, targeting, creativity, media mix, and integrated brand promotion strategies. Emphasizes responding to the challenges and opportunities presented by modern marketing trends.

courses.title: Embedded Design: Enabling Robotics
courses.description: Introduces the basics of digital design and programming for embedded systems through an integrated lecture and lab course. Covers digital design, object-oriented programming, using Linux, and controlling physical devices via software. The lab portion of this course offers hands-on experience at developing hardware and software to control a robotic device via an embedded systems platform.

courses.title: Introduction to Digital Design and Computer Architecture
courses.description: Covers the fundamentals of digital design topics including digital data representations and operations as well as combinational and sequential circuits design. Introduces various topics of computer organization, architecture, and microarchitecture applicable to modern computer engineering systems. Discusses principles of computer memory addressing. Applies the concept of hardware and software interface through programming in assembly language.

courses.title: Lab for EECE 2310
courses.description: Accompanies EECE 2310. Offers students an opportunity to design and implement various digital circuits that are essential for building computer systems. Utilizes a hardware design platform that is built around Field Programmable Gate Array (FPGA) technology. Covers simulation and testing of designs to solve practical engineering problems.

courses.title: Introduction to Linear Algebra and Probability for Data Science
courses.description: This course introduces the fundamentals of statistics, probability, and linear algebra. Covers random variables, frequency distributions, measures of central tendency, measures of dispersion, moments of a distribution, discrete and continuous probability distributions, chain rule, Bayes’ rule, correlation theory, basic sampling, matrix operations, trace of a matrix, norms, linear independence and ranks, inverse of a matrix, orthogonal matrices, range and null space of a matrix, the determinant of a matrix, positive semidefinite matrices, eigenvalues, and eigenvectors.

courses.title: Computer Vision
courses.description: Introduces topics such as image formation, segmentation, feature extraction, matching, shape recovery, dynamic scene analysis, and object recognition. Computer vision brings together imaging devices, computers, and sophisticated algorithms to solve problems in industrial inspection, autonomous navigation, human-computer interfaces, medicine, image retrieval from databases, realistic computer graphics rendering, document analysis, and remote sensing. The goal of computer vision is to make useful decisions about real physical objects and scenes based on sensed images. Computer vision is an exciting but disorganized field that builds on very diverse disciplines such as image processing, statistics, pattern recognition, control theory, system identification, physics, geometry, computer graphics, and learning theory. Requires good programming experience in Matlab or C++.

courses.title: Parallel Processing for Data Analytics
courses.description: Covers the fundamentals of parallel machine-learning algorithms, tailored specifically to learning tasks involving large data sets. Reviews methods for dealing with both large and high-dimensional data sets, emphasizing distributed implementations. Beyond covering the theory behind statistical data analysis, the course also offers a hands-on approach, using Spark as a development platform for parallel learning. Topics include, Apache Spark fundamentals, multithreaded/cluster execution, resilient distributed data structures, map-reduce operations, using key-value pairs, joins, convex optimization, gradient descent, linear regression, Gauss-Markov theorem, ridge and lasso regularization, feature selection, cross validation, variance vs. bias trade-off, classification, logistic regression, ROC curves and AUC, matrix and tensor factorization, graph-parallel algorithms and sparsity, Perceptron algorithm, and deep neural networks.

courses.title: Probabilistic System Modeling and Analysis
courses.description: Covers fundamentals of probabilistic system modeling, building toward techniques that allow analyzing complex stochastic systems in a tractable fashion. Modeling large and complex systems requires reasoning about probabilistic behavior at a large scale. Reviews classic topics like Markov chains, convergence to a steady state, renewal processes, renewal reward processes, the strong law of large numbers, and the elementary renewal theorem. Additional topics include the asymptotic behavior of probabilistic systems, including stochastic approximation/Robbins-Monro type algorithms, and ODE/fluid limits. Illustrates how these modeling techniques can be applied in modeling real systems and adaptive algorithms, including queueing systems, distributed systems, and online learning algorithms like stochastic gradient descent.

courses.title: Mobile and Wireless Networking
courses.description: Analyzes the working principles and practice of current and future mobile and wireless networks. Topics include overviews of different wireless and mobile systems and applications, 5G and beyond cellular networks, current and upcoming Wi-Fi standards, and emerging networking architectures. Covers theoretical foundations to computer simulation-based exercises. Designed with a research component involving a project that allows students to match classroom concepts to real-world problems. The overall objective is to enable the student to critically evaluate current and future wireless and mobile systems through an awareness of communication and networking theory, software protocols, and architectural know-how.

courses.title: Machine Learning with Small Data
courses.description: This course addresses machine learning in small-data regimes, focusing on methods that enable learning with limited supervision and scarce labeled data. Topics include formal learning theory, transfer learning, domain adaptation, weak supervision, zero- and few-shot learning, multi-task learning, and meta-learning. The course further explores data augmentation through data-driven and physics-based simulation, and introduces modern generative approaches for data-efficient representation learning. Students will engage with research papers, implement methods on shared compute infrastructure, and complete a final project. The course is intended for students with prior machine learning experience and Python proficiency. Deep learning experience is not necessary but beneficial.

courses.title: Making an Impact: "So What-Who Cares?"
courses.description: The “So What-Who Cares”? (SWWC) based approach is provided to students in this course through a “learn-by-doing” format. Thus, students will be required to make a number of presentations in different formats during the semester, and then to revise and repeat their presentations in response to critiques from both the instructor and other students in the course. In that regard, critical listening to talks by others is a very useful tool for learning, so students will be expected to critique each other and are also required to attend out-of-class seminars and submit brief reports on them. The “So What-Who Cares?” theme should underlie critiques of these seminars as well. In summary, this course will help students improve their ’soft skills’, which, at times, can be a deciding factor in a competitive setting. Also, the topics are planned to extend the reach and clarity of a student’s research work which will improve its external visibility.

courses.title: Research
courses.description: Offers students an opportunity to conduct full-time research under faculty supervision.

courses.title: Research
courses.description: Offers students an opportunity to conduct full-time research under faculty supervision. May be repeated without limit.

courses.title: Ecological and Conservation Genomics
courses.description: Offers an overview of ecological and conservation genetics, an interdisciplinary science that focuses on understanding the processes that determine genetic diversity at the individual to population level. Focuses on fundamental concepts in evolutionary ecology and population and quantitative genetics, then applies those concepts to solving real-world problems in conservation science. Covers harvested populations, inbreeding, climate change, introduced species, conservation of threatened and endangered species, adaptation, and habitat restoration. Exposes students to multiple sides of these issues and the science that underpins them. Offers students an opportunity to develop the R programming skills required to analyze the complex data sets that often emerge when addressing cutting-edge questions in genetics. Includes writing and coding exercises and mathematical derivations. Emphasizes critical thinking and problem solving.

courses.title: Population Dynamics
courses.description: Offers a comprehensive overview of the mathematical and computational concepts needed to construct dynamical models. Lectures describe how to mathematically derive and model the effects of species interactions, space, disease, and environmental variability in order to understand the dynamics of populations in a changing world. Emphasizes the mathematical tools required to analyze the dynamical behavior of models (e.g., stability, invasion, graphical, and numerical analyses) and validate their predictions using empirical data (e.g., via maximum likelihood and optimization methods). Tutorials demonstrate how to implement, analyze, and test models using the free R programming language.

courses.title: Introduction to Engineering Co-op Education
courses.description: Introduces engineering students to the cooperative education program and assists them through self-assessment in relation to the engineering profession. Offers students an opportunity to develop job search and career management skills and to learn about the co-op program. Analyzes how personality, values, skills, and interests relate to the co-op search and shape work experiences. Employer and student panels assist students exploring employment opportunities. Also introduces students to the attributes of a global engineer and the possibility of working globally.

courses.title: College Writing 1
courses.description: Offers students an opportunity to develop written communication skills and basic research techniques in preparation for English-language college writing in their majors. Incorporates reading, research, and critical thinking in the development of expository writing—the kind of objective, audience-directed prose used in college and the workplace to explain and defend ideas. Emphasizes planning, drafting, revising, and correct citation in essays, along with development of focus, organization, and paragraph/sentence structure. In addition to in-class assignments and peer-review activities, includes extended essays developed outside of class.Requires a grade of C or higher in order to receive credit and continue on to ENG 1107

courses.title: Lab for ENG 1105
courses.description: Requires students to analyze and draft writing assignments from topics covered in ENG 1105.

courses.title: Speak Now: Gender, Genre, & Storytelling in Taylor Swift's Eras
courses.description: From confessional ballads to retellings of gothic romance classics, this course explores how women’s literary and cultural influences on genre and narrative have shaped the artistry of Taylor Swift’s ten eras. We will discover how Swift harnesses a history of women’s storytelling to connect with global audiences’ emotions - across Fearless’ joy, Midnights’ shame, Reputation’s rage, and Red’s passion. We will also consider the role of identity, including race, gender, and sexuality, in the success and messaging of Swift’s music. Students will have the opportunity to reflect on the connections between their own identities and experiences and how they connect with Swift’s songs.

courses.title: Introduction to Literary Studies
courses.description: Introduces the diverse fields that comprise literary studies for English majors and minors. Surveys the methods and topics of English literary and textual studies, including a wide range of media (e.g., images, film, and graphic narrative). Explores strategies for reading, interpreting, and theorizing about texts, including how race, gender, sexuality, class, and colonialism are represented in literary texts, other media, and scholarship. Focuses on developing skills in thinking analytically, writing clearly about complex ideas, and conducting research.

courses.title: Contemporary Fiction
courses.description: Examines British and American writers from 1945 to the present, including such figures as Lessing, Burgess, Pynchon, Morrison, Kingston, and Erdrich.

courses.title: Professional Writing and Design
courses.description: Introduces students to professional writing, a distinct career field in which writing itself is the primary work. Uses a project-based approach that offers students an opportunity to write for clients in the community. Focuses on analyzing rhetorical situations (including audiences, purposes, and constraints) and designing documents to meet specific needs. Presents editorial processes, practices for collaborating with subject matter experts, and theories of usability and instructional design.

courses.title: Film and Text
courses.description: Studies the similarities and differences between literary texts and film versions of those texts and the interrelations between film and literature as forms of cultural expression in particular places and contexts. Visits to museums, festivals, city walks, historical sites, or other field trips connect local, regional, and national contexts and offer students an opportunity to compare how texts and films represent these material spaces through different aesthetic forms. May be repeated twice up to a total of 12 semester hours.

courses.title: Major Seminar
courses.description: Offers English majors in-depth study organized around an important critical question in English studies. Presents a framework, in a seminar setting, for students to examine disciplinary theories, methods, and practices. Offers students an opportunity to reflect upon their experiences in and paths through the major in seminar projects.

courses.title: Topics in Early Literatures
courses.description: Focuses on a particular theme, author, genre (such as epic or romance), or other aspect of premodern literatures (such as representations of gender and sexuality and/or race). Selected texts may include classical literary works or literatures of the Middle Ages and/or the Early Modern period.

courses.title: 20th- and 21st-Century Literatures
courses.description: Focuses on a particular topic, theme, author, or genre in 20th- or 21st-century British and/or American literature. Emphasizes historical and cultural contexts. May be repeated once.

courses.title: Topics in Shakespeare
courses.description: Examines a focused topic, theme, or critical approach to Shakespeare.

courses.title: Topics in 19th-Century British Literature
courses.description: Focuses on a specific topic (e.g., nature, gender, industrialism, empire) or genre (e.g., autobiography, the sensation novel, lyric poetry) in nineteenth-century British literature. May be repeated up to two times.

courses.title: Topics in 20th- and 21st-Century Literatures
courses.current_step: ENGL Graduate Department Curriculum Committee Chair
courses.description: Examines an issue or issues in 20th- and 21st-century literatures such as gender and sexuality, surrealism in modern and contemporary poetry, naturalism and the city in the modern novel. autobiography, and race and ethnicity. May be repeated twice.

courses.title: Topics in Renaissance Literature
courses.current_step: ENGL Graduate Department Curriculum Committee Chair
courses.description: Considers specific topics in the literature of the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries, such as the sonnet sequence, Renaissance women, and utopian and travel literature. May be repeated twice.

courses.title: Topics in 19th-Century Literatures
courses.current_step: ENGL Graduate Department Curriculum Committee Chair
courses.description: Focuses on health humanities, slave narratives, American literature, British literature, transatlanticism, and gender and racial capitalism in 19th-century literature. May be repeated twice.

courses.title: Topics in Criticism and Theory
courses.current_step: ENGL Graduate Department Curriculum Committee Chair
courses.description: Examines such topics in critical theory as narratology, cultural criticism, representation, reader response, feminism, postcolonial studies, queer and decolonial theories, and theories and criticism in rhetoric and composition studies. May be repeated twice.

courses.title: Topics in Literary Study
courses.current_step: ENGL Graduate Department Curriculum Committee Chair
courses.description: Focuses on literature on a thematic, formal, or generic basis. Varies by instructor.

courses.title: Topics in Rhetoric
courses.current_step: ENGL Graduate Department Curriculum Committee Chair
courses.description: Focuses on specialized topics in rhetoric, such as visual rhetoric, rhetorical criticism, rhetoric of science, issues in contemporary rhetorical theory, and rhetoric and cultural studies. Varies by semester. May be repeated without limit.

courses.title: Introduction to Digital Humanities
courses.current_step: ENGL Graduate Department Curriculum Committee Chair
courses.description: Offers a critical orientation to the tools, methods, and intellectual history of the digital humanities (DH). Explores key questions such as what debates are (re)shaping DH in this moment; what central theories lead humanities scholars to experiment with computational, geospatial, and network methodologies; how visualization can illuminate literature, history, writing, and other humanities subjects; and how new modes of research and publication might influence our teaching. Balances theory and praxis: Successful students come away with a well-grounded understanding of the DH field and a set of foundational skills to support their future research. No prior technical expertise is required to take the course, but students should be willing to experiment with new skills.

courses.title: Topics in Writing
courses.current_step: ENGL Graduate Department Curriculum Committee Chair
courses.description: May include the following topics: literacy and literacies; basic writing; issues of gender, race, and class in the classroom; writing assessment; or collaborative learning. May be repeated without limit.

courses.title: Effective Presentation Communications Methods
courses.description: This is a workshop for learners who seek to develop advanced skills in presentation communications for diverse audiences. Explores how to identify the target audience and the goal of the communication. Discusses methods for adjusting the message to the target audience and for increasing the oral and visual communication signal-to-noise ratio. Offers learners an opportunity to apply the theories and practices to real-world problems.

courses.title: Organizing Academic Success: AI for Personalized Learning
courses.description: This course is designed for learners seeking to leverage AI in managing complex coursework, projects, and extracurricular commitments.Students will learn to use AI tools to organize academic materials, enhance study practices, and collaborate effectively, while understanding AI's broader applications in engineering and beyond.

courses.title: AI Evaluation
courses.description: Examines rigorous AI evaluation protocols through hands-on assignments and explores their application across various domains, including medicine, law, chemistry, finance, and drug discovery. Emphasizes principles of AI evaluation and their role in assessing AI systems across disciplines.

courses.title: Prompt Engineering: How to Communicate and Interact with LLMs
courses.description: Introduces prompt engineering and its applications, focusing on effective communication with Large Language Models (LLMs) for various tasks. Combines theoretical concepts with hands-on exercises to develop practical skills in leveraging LLMs.

courses.title: Leading Self and Others
courses.description: Offers students an opportunity to develop knowledge, self-awareness, skills, and attitudes in order to influence others, lead teams, and contribute as productive team members by leading from the middle. Includes engagement with an outside organization to scope a project. Explores how to influence others by leading teams and producing reliable and practical value to a sponsoring organization; applying methods to define clear objectives, desired outcomes, and customer needs in addressing problems requiring breadth and depth in scientific and technical realms; and articulating the purpose, goals, and milestones of an experiential leadership challenge project.

courses.title: Experiential Challenge Project 1
courses.description: Serves as the first half of a project in technology commercialization. Includes work with a sponsoring organization or employer to improve a process or develop a project of significant organizational value, with attention to market impact, technological and engineering depth, and leadership development.

courses.title: Experiential Challenge Project 2
courses.description: Continues ENLR 5660.Serves as the second half of a project in technology commercialization. Offers students an opportunity to develop and present a plan for the demonstration of a marketable technology product or prototype. Includes work with a sponsoring organization or employer to improve a process or develop a project of significant organizational value, with attention to market impact, technological and engineering depth, and leadership development.

courses.title: Fundamentals of Solar Photovoltaic Energy Conversion
courses.description: Focuses on the principles and working fundamentals of photovoltaic (PV) energy conversion, while emphasizing currently available solar technologies. Studies the semiconductor processes and advanced characterization theories. Examines design, fabrication, characterization of the PV modules, and different generations of solar cells and their properties. Advanced topics include thin film cells, compound semiconductors multijunction, multiband cells, spectral conversion, and introduces organic devices. Offers insight about the energy consumption crisis, sustainable energy sources, PV system components, and solar markets. Also discusses issues relating to PV systems, economics, and sustainability.

courses.title: Research
courses.current_step: MEIE Graduate Department Curriculum Committee Chair
courses.description: Offers students an opportunity to conduct full-time research under faculty supervision.

courses.title: Global Entrepreneurship
courses.description: Offers students the opportunity to learn how social and sustainable enterprises start and grow in different global contexts. Introduces the basic concepts and tools associated with small business leadership.Students experience ﬁrsthand the unique challenges entrepreneurs and innovators face when building and sustaining impact organizations in economically disadvantaged and low-resource settings. Through a consulting experience, students have the opportunity to learn how to make strategic, culturally-aligned recommendations that help organizations grow their impact in a ﬁnancially sustainable way.

courses.title: Maritime Design & Field Technology
courses.description: Explores CAD applications in the maritime industry, 3D modeling of marine environments, and field deployment of measurement systems. Examines historical and real-time modeling data, introduces metric and imperial measurement systems, and covers the use of remote sensors for coastal monitoring.

courses.title: Maritime Safety and Emergency Response
courses.current_step: Provost Continuing Education Module Oversight Group
courses.description: Explores maritime safety, emergency response, and crisis management. Provides learners opportunities to practice CPR, AED, and First Aid. Discusses maritime crisis protocols and covers NASBLA and USCG credentials for safe boating and coastal navigation operations.

courses.title: Professional Maritime Communications
courses.current_step: Provost Continuing Education Module Oversight Group
courses.description: Introduces professional skills for the maritime industry including resume building, professional writing, interviewing, and networking. Provides an opportunity for learners to create industry-specific application materials, practice effective workplace communication, and establish professional networks within the maritime sector.

courses.title: Applied Hydrogeology
courses.description: Covers the origin, distribution, and flow of groundwater in permeable sediments and bedrock; hydrological and geological characteristics of aquifers; regional flow systems emphasizing rock structure, stratigraphy, and other aspects of the geological environment; principles of hydrogeologic mapping and analysis; and introduces well testing and well hydraulics. Uses methods of collecting data about the physical distribution and properties of water and its interaction with geologic materials in the subsurface, including its chemical composition, and mathematical models to interpret the direction and velocity of groundwater flow. Considers remediation strategies for dealing with contaminated water in the subsurface.

courses.title: Wetlands
courses.description: Presents an interdisciplinary overview of the physical, biological, and cultural aspects of wetlands. Topics covered include definitions, classification systems, origins, human use, and natural processes of wetland environments. Offers students an opportunity to learn about wetland hydrology, soils, and vegetation and their relationship to ecosystem processes, societal values, and management. Includes reading and analyzing the scientific literature and conducting in-class activities.

courses.title: Climate and Atmospheric Change
courses.description: Offers an in-depth view of the processes that drive change in Earth's climate system. Examines the modern climate system and how and why climate changes through time. Introduces the tools used to explore past climates and changes, and explores the long-term and short-term controls on the climate system. Also introduces the application of climate models to develop future climate projections. Offers students an opportunity to obtain hands-on experience analyzing and interpreting climate data and model output.

courses.title: Experiential Learning: Internship & Co-op
courses.description: Offers students an opportunity to gain experience in a field they would like to explore.

courses.title: Professional Development Co-op
courses.description: Introduces graduate students to the cooperative education program. Offers students an opportunity to develop job-search and career-management skills. Students perform discipline-specific assessments of their workplace skills, interests, and values and discuss how they impact personal career choices. Covers how to develop field/industry-specific materials, including a professional-style resumé and cover letter, and introduces students to career portfolios. Additional topics include ethics, professional behaviors, workplace culture, and proper interviewing techniques. Familiarizes students with workplace issues relative to their field of study while outlining co-op policies, procedures, and expectations of the cooperative education program and employers. This course does not count toward degree requirements.

courses.title: Financial Literacy and Financial Literacy Leadership for Social Capital Wealth-building Inequities
courses.description: This course will familiarize students with financial literacy, and financial literacy leadership to address social wealth-building capital inequity.

courses.title: Fundamentals of Financial Math and Financial Markets
courses.description: Focuses on financial mathematics and financial markets, combining theoretical foundations with selected empirical and computational applications. Introduces core tools from linear algebra (matrix operations, vector spaces); calculus (differentiation and integration); optimization (Lagrange multipliers, inequality constraints, Kuhn-Tucker conditions, and basic convex analysis); and probability and stochastic processes (axioms of probability, random variables, joint distributions, and common stochastic models). Involves application of these methods to quantitative finance problems such as time-value-of-money- and regression-based market relationships, mean-variance portfolio optimization, simulation-based risk/return analysis, and introductory derivative-pricing models (e.g., Black–Scholes). Emphasizes implementation using modern computational tools to build quantitative and analytical skills for financial modeling and rigorous financial market analysis.

courses.title: Corporate Governance
courses.description: Examines the conflicts of interest associated with the separation of ownership and control of the corporation. Applies agency theory and practice to the issues raised and draws on seminal works in the finance and economics literature that influence the current debate in this area. Discusses legal and ethical considerations that are especially important in corporate-control issues. Uses cases to illustrate the theories discussed and data and software from the Wharton Research Data Services database to measure financial relationships related to corporate governance.

courses.title: Risk Parity Investing
courses.description: Introduces risk parity investing. Risk parity is an asset allocation approach that considers the risk contribution from each asset and overall portfolio risk, which can lead to more stable portfolio returns over time. Exposes students to risk parity, with and without reallocation and leverage, and offers them an opportunity to deepen their understanding of diversification, modern portfolio theory, and asset allocation.

courses.title: Futures Market Trading Strategies
courses.description: Introduces futures market trading strategies. These trading strategies provide an alternative approach to security selection and portfolio construction, offering investors returns that are uncorrelated to traditional stock and bond portfolios. Covers global macro trading, trend following, and carry strategies. Includes other topics such as constructing trading strategies, exploring the effects of leverage, and incorporating strategies into a traditional portfolio.

courses.title: Risk Arbitrage
courses.description: Introduces risk arbitrage, a strategy that profits from price gaps during mergers and acquisitions. Explores how to analyze deals, estimate success odds, and build portfolios. Offers students an opportunity to test real-world strategies and weigh risks vs. rewards in alternative investments.

courses.title: Connections and Decisions
courses.description: Introduces first-year students who have not yet declared a major to the various disciplines across the university. Prompts students to fully explore and engage with campus resources, helps them to develop the analytical and critical thinking skills necessary to choose a major, and offers them grounding in the culture and values of the college and the university community.

courses.title: Short Topics In Game Design
courses.description: Focuses on a specific topic of relevance to the domain of Game Design. May be repeated up to 7 times for a maximum of 8 semester hours. Topics vary each semester.

courses.title: Product Design and Human Impact
courses.description: Offers students an integrative opportunity to learn product design from a combined technical and human perspective. Topics studied include a historical view of how technology has shaped society, how society has understood the role of technology, and how the values of the designer affect the technologies that are developed for the human experience. Requires students to identify a human need and to design and prototype a product solution. Students will present their work including placement of the problem and proposed solution in a human context.

courses.title: Making Fundamentals in Design and Fabrication
courses.description: Utilizes numerous, accessible, low-to-medium fidelity fabrication methods used in design and fabrication prototyping. Offers students an opportunity to develop in-depth knowledge to investigate multiple manufacturing and assembly methods and learn best practices, with a goal of demonstrating the ability to choose the best technique from among the options presented. Coursework consists of individual and team-based hands-on experiences using these tools to design and build prototypes via a suite of weekly/biweekly design-build assignments. Designed for interested nonengineering students.

courses.title: Energy Systems: Science, Technology, and Sustainability
courses.description: Offers students an opportunity to obtain a sound scientific, technological, and economic understanding of our modern energy system and the challenge of energy sustainability. Covers principles of energy, work, and thermodynamics; technologies from supply and demand side, including extraction of primary energy, conversion into fuels and electricity, important energy end-uses, and energy losses; fossil, nuclear power plants, and renewable energy technologies (wind, solar, wave, hydro, geothermal, biofuels); transmission and distribution for electricity and fossil fuels; energy demand by buildings, transportation, and industry, emphasizing efficient technologies; sustainability concepts, including net energy/exergy analysis and life-cycle assessment, energy-related emissions, decentralized generation, smart grids, district heating, and net-zero energy facilities.

courses.title: N.U.in Program International Leadership
courses.description: Presents a formal recognition of sustained leadership and civic engagement within a first-semester international study environment. Emphasizes community-building, representation, and responsibility within a diverse global cohort during a significant academic and cultural transition. Topics include student governance structures, collaborative decision-making, event development, peer advocacy, and communication between students and institutional partners in an international context. The course consists of structured participation in a student-led council that shapes community life at an N.U.in site and supports inclusive engagement across the cohort. The badge documents early evidence of leadership practice, civic responsibility, and global competency through Northeastern University’s verified digital credentialing system.

courses.title: Off-Campus Community Ambassador Student Leadership Program - Boston Campus
courses.description: Provides learners in the Boston Community Ambassador program an opportunity to develop their understanding and application of civic and university knowledge to support the holistic needs of college students who are living off campus. Focuses on peer education and advising.

courses.title: Globalization and Intelligence Issues
courses.description: Highlights key themes currently developing within international intelligence liaison relationships. Explores trends toward homogenization of intelligence and other law enforcement and security initiatives. Examines international standardization among a widening group of partners and how these processes may establish viable frameworks and operational parameters for intelligence liaison arrangements, together with addressing counterintelligence and other security considerations. Analyzes current threats and opportunities posed by AI and other emerging technologies utilized by national security agencies and their partner organizations.

courses.title: National Security Law and Policy
courses.description: Examines the various elements of national power and their application in advancing U.S. interests.
