Explores the distribution of national security powers among the three branches of government. Identifies challenges of a distributive structure of national security power and the complex ethical considerations involved in the use of national security powers. Offers students an opportunity to recognize the synergy a multidisciplined approach affords by analyzing current strategy and policy.

courses.title: Multilateral Governance─Law and Diplomacy Strategy
courses.description: Examines contemporary theories of multilateral governance and institutional design, as well as current legal authority and its inherent limitations. Reviews how theoretical perspectives are operationalized in core institutional systems such as the U.N. Security Council and international financial institutions. Examines how current international disputes are resolved and the roles that AI and international ethical constructs play in resolving disputes.

courses.title: Foreign Affairs and Foreign Policy Analysis
courses.description: Examines how nations develop, implement, and evaluate foreign policy by integrating theoretical models with practical applications. Studies decision making; managing bilateral relationships; and contemporary foreign policy issues including strategic competition, economic diplomacy, and crisis response. Focuses on evidence-based analysis and professional communication through case studies, simulations, and applied research on real-world foreign policy challenges. Offers students an opportunity to develop advanced analytical skills.

courses.title: Sexpionage: The History of Sex in Spying from the Ancient World to the Cold War
courses.description: Drawing from a wide variety of published and unpublished primary and secondary sources, supplemented by modern theoretical and social science perspectives, literature, and films, this intersession course explores the elusive history of sexpionage—the use of sex in spying in human history. After a short introduction, there will be three 90-100 minute lectures: 1. Sex and spying among the Ancients through to the dawn of the 20th century; 2. The birth of modern sexpionage in World War I with Mata Hari through World War II. And 3. Modern Cold War and post-Cold War case histories.

courses.title: Ethnoreligious Communities and the Making of the Modern Middle East
courses.description: Examines ethnoreligious communities in the modern Middle East from the late Ottoman period to the present. Analyzes community-state relations, nationalism, identity formation, and political change across the region. Draws on historical case studies and political analysis to explore community life in Islamic societies and in the Jewish State of Israel.

courses.title: An Introductory Journey from Data to Evidence
courses.description: Explores the process for how to navigate the analytical journey from real world data to real world evidence using the Observational Medical Medical Outcomes Partnership (OMOP) Common Data Model, Observational Health Data Sciences and Informatics (OHDSI) tools and scientific best practices. This is a continuing education course and is not for credit.

courses.title: Healthcare Simulation
courses.description: Offers an opportunity to learn about simulation-based learning experiences (SBE) as a teaching, learning and assessment tool. Covers educational theory that supports the use of SBE in health professions' education, a review of the literature in healthcare simulation, and the standards of best practice in SBE. Provides an opportunity to create an original simulation scenario for use within a course. Offers application of concepts and simulation skills practice.

courses.title: A Provider's Pathway to Supporting Patients with Substance Use Disorder
courses.description: Presents critical topics at the intersection of substance use disorder (SUD), clinical practice, health equity, and patient-centered care. Incorporates modules addressing the landscape of SUD in the United States, the role of stigma as a barrier to care, evidence-based treatment options and harm reduction, social determinants of health, and culturally responsive care. Emphasizes public health approaches for the conceptualization and treatment of SUD. Provides students the opportunity to enhance knowledge, attitudes, and skills related to the provision of high-quality, non-stigmatizing care for people who use substances.

courses.title: Internship
courses.description: Provides students with an opportunity for research internship work. May be repeated up to four times.

courses.title: Art, Story, & Health: Narrative Health Psychology in Critical Times
courses.description: Students will explore the role of storytelling in making sense of unexpected disruptions in our everyday lives and their relationship to health. Health psychology studies how psychological, behavioral, and socio-cultural factors contribute to physical health and the body. Narrative health psychology additionally posits that we actively construct the world (including health and illness) through narratives, and we live through the stories told. Narratives weave shared ways of making sense of the world and experience; they are at the same time personal, relational, and grounded in social contexts. Through listening to stories represented through voice, text and artistic media - we understand how others experience, make meaning and learn from disruptions related to their health and that of people close to them. Considering the disruptions in daily activities, future plans, social connections and general uncertainty during the COVID-19 pandemic, this learning experience will give students an opportunity to take time to reflect and explore what they are learning during this time - through narrative practices such as writing, painting and photography.

courses.title: Honors Discovery Experience
courses.description: Offers students an opportunity to take full advantage of the John Martinson Honors Program and to foster a sense of community within the first-year honors experience. Students explore the goals of the John Martinson Honors Program through a variety of experiential learning activities, such as taking part in a theme-based living/learning community, learning through an interdisciplinary perspective, advancing their global mindset, and making an impact in the communities that they inhabit.

courses.title: Honors Community Engagement
courses.description: Provides students with opportunities to participate in and reflect upon thematic learning experiences fostered within a peer community of curious, interdisciplinary scholars. Students must meet a standard minimum participation established by Honors.

courses.title: Honors Self-Directed Exploration
courses.description: Provides students with an opportunity to engage with and reflect upon a non-credit learning experience whose outcomes are in alignment with the John Martinson Honors Program mission and goals, namely empowering students' intellectual curiosity and facilitating their exploration of academic, professional, and civic competencies. A list of approved options can be found on the John Martinson Honors Program website.

courses.title: Honors BLUEPRINT for Student Leadership Development
courses.description: Explores foundational leadership concepts through the lens of the LEAD360 Circular Leadership Model. Invites emerging leaders to examine their values, analyze inclusive leadership practices, and reflect on the leadership styles of peers. Offers the opportunity to engage in a series of facilitated discussions and activities to develop a personalized leadership framework that supports future engagement in the Honors community and beyond.

courses.title: Honors Interdisciplinarity
courses.description: Examines how interdisciplinary knowledge and skills can be applied to address complex issues across academic, professional, and public contexts. Focuses on communicating and collaborating in group settings and developing critical reflection through three signature Honors experiences.

courses.title: Strategic Workforce Planning
courses.description: Explores the development of talent management programs required to effectively execute corporate strategy. Effective workforce planning and implementation are essential to the maintenance of an organization’s competitive advantage and the successful execution of organizational strategy. Reviews topics in human resource planning, gap analysis, overcoming implementation barriers, and promoting the change process.

courses.title: Science of Play
courses.description: Examines the scholarship of play. Explores the role and function, benefits, and barriers of play in child development. Topics include the background and significance of play in history; the role of play as a predictor of academic and social functioning; and the use of play in character/moral development and to prevent, intervene, and treat trauma. Explores clinical and nonclinical implications of play, as well as the physiological and social implications of play, using contemporary research on brain science and development. Combines classroom learning with fieldwork experiences throughout the local Boston community and independent research on the role of play as prevention, intervention, and treatment. Students develop service-based research projects with community partners to address key questions related to the science of play.

courses.title: Computation and Visualization
courses.description: Covers the basics of data wrangling and data visualization. Provides students the opportunity to read, clean, select, and manipulate structured data. Introduces visualization charts and techniques that reveal information, patterns, interactions, and comparisons, combining theory and hands-on activities to extract meaningful insights from the data.

courses.title: Data Management for Analytics
courses.description: Introduces the fundamental concepts and emerging technologies in database design and modeling, database systems, data storage and data governance. Presents a balanced theory-practice focus and covers entity relationship model and UML model, relational model, relational databases, Structured Query Language, and two flavors of NoSQL databases in MongoDB and Neo4j graph database.

courses.title: Algorithms and Optimization
courses.description: Introduces algorithms and their uses in industry. Provides a general overview of algorithms. Introduces operations research, linear programming, graphs and graph algorithms, greedy algorithms, dynamic programming algorithms, network flow algorithms, and computational intractability, and explores metaheuristic algorithms.

courses.title: Data Analytics
courses.description: Introduces data mining concepts and statistics/machine learning models and techniques for analyzing and discovering knowledge from large data sets that occur in engineering. Examines data mining case studies that are drawn from manufacturing and business.

courses.title: Sensor Analytics
courses.description: Covers fundamentals of different types of sensors commonly used in production and manufacturing environments and basic principles of operations of different sensors. Provides a solid theoretical foundation for analyzing and processing experimental data in both discrete- and continuous-time domains.

courses.title: Cybermanufacturing Systems
courses.description: Introduces the foundational concepts of industrial automation and its layered structure from the physical plant floor to institutional planning platforms. Discusses networking concepts based on real-life case studies.

courses.title: Robotics and Automation
courses.description: Introduces the practice of robotics and automation systems in manufacturing. Takes an interdisciplinary approach to cover the foundational topics in this field. Covers systems and application centered methodology. Each module involves concepts and projects on fundamentals and practical aspects.

courses.title: Introduction to Industrial Engineering
courses.description: Provides an overview of the history of industrial engineering and of the most common methods that industrial engineers use to solve problems and design efficient processes. The emphasis is on how these methods are used to study, improve, and/or optimize a product or process. Topics include work design, ergonomic design, engineering statistics, quality engineering, engineering economics, project management, and process optimization. Also discusses the design of the production processes, facilities, and material handling systems. Studies applications in manufacturing, product design, and service industries. Laboratory experiments and written reports are required.

courses.title: Deep Learning for AI
courses.description: Provides an in-depth exploration of modern deep learning architectures and methodologies that drive today’s AI systems. Examines both foundational and advanced models, with attention to theoretical understanding and practical implementation through programming assignments and projects. Topics include convolutional and recurrent networks, generative models, transformers, diffusion models, multimodal learning, and emerging approaches such as world models. Emphasizes mathematical rigor and real-world applications across computer vision, natural language processing, and generative AI.

courses.title: Research
courses.current_step: MEIE Graduate Department Curriculum Committee Chair
courses.description: Offers students an opportunity to conduct full-time research under faculty supervision.

courses.title: Research
courses.current_step: MEIE Graduate Department Curriculum Committee Chair
courses.description: Offers students an opportunity to conduct full-time research under faculty supervision. May be repeated without limit.

courses.title: Visual Storytelling
courses.description: Explores narrative sequence and story development in a variety of story architectures and media combinations. Provides practical methodologies for detecting and articulating the stories behind datasets and how to communicate data findings in visual, oral, and written contexts. Instruction will cover select topics in data-driven storytelling projects across industry sectors and provide practical tools for navigating the often-competing demands of rigorous analysis and accessible narrative and storytelling.

courses.title: Introduction to Immersive Media
courses.description: Immersive technologies create distinct experiences by merging the physical world with a digital or simulated reality. This module introduces three forms of immersive media—augmented reality (AR), virtual reality (VR), and 360 video. Participants will get an overview of the tools, industry sectors, and professional applications for these immersive technologies by learning the fundamental software tools, development methodologies, and production techniques.

courses.title: Creative Coding
courses.description: This hands-on module, which requires no prior coding experience, offers an accessible introduction to the fundamentals of development and creative design for learners who are new to programming or using code for design. Creative coding explores the interdisciplinary spaces between technology, design, and media across industry sectors. Participants will learn how to use javascript to make original works of creative code that move, interact and unfold over time. Through lectures, hands-on exercises, and modular projects, explores web-based design and programming solutions for creating interactive user experiences.

courses.title: Facilitating Creative Collaboration
courses.description: Successful leaders leverage a variety of techniques and tools to discover, understand, and maximize the ways in which teams interact, communicate, and collaborate. This module explores the different types of roles that individual contributors may take in team-based environments, methods for navigating challenging situations to improve team productivity, and techniques for strengthening leadership, communication, and collaborative skills.

courses.title: Intercultural and Intergenerational Communication
courses.description: Effective leaders require keen awareness, agility, and strategies when working in team-based professional settings. This module examines intercultural and intergenerational communication between colleagues in the workplace. Instruction and small group work are designed to help participants achieve cross-cultural competence in mediated communication, interpersonal and group dynamics, and organizational climates. Attendees will learn how to better exchange meaningful and unambiguous information across cultural and generational boundaries in a manner that maximizes understanding.

courses.title: Data Visualization
courses.description: Defines the information visualization domain and advances principles and methods for the effective visual representation of data. Presents the perceptual and cognitive tasks enabled by visualizations, studies an extensive range of visualization models, illustrates good and bad practices in visualization with real-world examples, and introduces concepts in computer programming in an information visualization context. The content is designed to foster moderate technical learning of applications and software.

courses.title: The Arts and Social Change
courses.description: Explores how artists, practitioners, and educators use theatre and the arts to address social and political conflict across diversity of contexts, issues, and locations and within both traditional and nontraditional settings. Examines how the arts have challenged social and political structures and how performance can be used in the community as a tool for social change. Discusses and challenges the role of the arts in society. Explores the work of community-based artists and educators who, within their specific contexts, transform and create new theatre techniques as a way of addressing social change. Offers students an opportunity to work with a local community organization to design a short performance project that addresses a topical issue.

courses.title: Research
courses.description: Offers students an opportunity to conduct full-time research under faculty supervision.

courses.title: Research Essentials - Undergraduate
courses.description: Engages students in the foundational elements of academic research. Emphasizes research literacy, library research tools and attendance at campus research events. Introduces skills for navigating academic research environments in preparation for further academic and professional development.

courses.title: Mentored Research - Undergraduate
courses.current_step: Provost Continuing Education Module Oversight Group Hold
courses.description: Engages students in intermediate-level research experiences within their field of study. Emphasizes application of research ethics, safety training, and methodological competencies through mentored research and scholarship. Develops skills in research presentation and scholarly communication with at least two formal or informal presentations. Prepares students for leadership opportunities and advanced research engagement across Northeastern’s global research network.

courses.title: Independent Research - Undergraduate
courses.current_step: Provost Continuing Education Module Oversight Group Hold
courses.description: Engages students in student-led research experiences within their field of study. Emphasizes leadership, independent inquiry, and dissemination through independent scholarship. Develops advanced skills in research project management, peer mentorship, and research communication, with presentation or publication.

courses.title: Advanced Research Scholar - Undergraduate
courses.current_step: Provost Continuing Education Module Oversight Group Hold
courses.description: Engages students in comprehensive research mastery, combining advanced inquiry, leadership, dissemination, and real-world application. Badge earners lead or co-lead scholarly projects with external recognition, contribute to community engagement, publish or present at professional venues, and mentor junior researchers. This badge certifies readiness for graduate programs, research fellowships, and roles in research settings.

courses.title: Mentored Research - Undergraduate
courses.description: Earners of this badge have actively participated in research or scholarship within their desired field. They have developed intermediate research competencies, demonstrated proficiency in at least one research methodology relevant to their discipline, and completed discipline-specific research ethics and safety training. Badge earners have presented their work in at least one setting (formal or informal) and contributed meaningfully to a research project under faculty mentorship. This badge signals growing expertise, preparing students for advanced research opportunities and future leadership roles.

courses.title: Special Topics in Interdisciplinary Studies
courses.description: Explores rotating themes in interdisciplinary studies, integrating perspectives and methods from diverse fields of inquiry. May be repeated twice.

courses.title: Community-Based and Participatory Action Research
courses.description: Aims to equip students with the skills for social research in intersectional issues of race and ethnicity. Examines how to carry out research with communities, engage with ethical considerations, and interrogate the historical and contemporary impact of research on communities of color. Familiarizes students with methodologies developed by oppressed communities that challenge the racialized and gendered inequalities underlying the production and dissemination of knowledge. Enables students to draw on insights from Ethnic Studies, Critical Health Studies, Disability Studies, and Women’s Studies to examine the intersections between race, nation, gender, sexuality and (dis)ability. Teaches students how to utilize interviews, focus groups, participant observation, and arts-based methods in the context of community-based, anti-oppressive research. Offers students the opportunity to design a senior thesis research project.

courses.title: Business Intelligence Innovation Challenge
courses.description: Presents a challenge that requires earners to use a combination of innovation, secondary source research and entrepreneurial thinking to overcome. Emphasizes the tools needed to address new venture creation fundamentals. Offers learners a unique blend of critical and creative thinking in a competitive, team-based environment.

courses.title: History, Culture, and Economics in Social Innovation & Entrepreneurship
courses.description: Understanding context is fundamental to developing context-speciﬁc and culturally relevant solutions to complex social problems. This course offers students the opportunity to learn the historical context, cultural complexity, and economic history that underpins present-day social problems in a speciﬁc city or country. The course uses a global learning experience to immerse students in the local culture and to learn about the complex history from the lived experience of local people and social innovators and entrepreneurs. who are passionate about driving change.

courses.title: Leading Data-Intensive Projects
courses.description:
In today’s data-driven organizations, successful projects depend on strong collaboration between business leaders, analysts, and technology teams.Leading Data-Intensive Projectsequips you with the skills to bridge the communication and knowledge gap between these groups, ensuring data initiatives deliver real business value. This course focuses on two critical areas where projects often struggle: the relationship between Technology, Clients, and Analysts, and the fundamentals of database platforms and related technologies.

Through a combination of group projects, individual assignments, and real-world case discussions, you’ll experience the common challenges of leading complex, data-intensive initiatives. A core component of the course is developing and presenting a clear, actionable Business Requirements Document that translates business needs into technical requirements. You’ll leave the course with practical experience in managing the frustrations, negotiations, and breakthroughs that come with leading projects at the intersection of data, business strategy, and technology execution.

courses.title: Exploring Marketing: Concepts and Careers
courses.description: Introduces the fundamentals of marketing, communication strategies, and marketing career exploration. Explores traditional and digital marketing channels, branding essentials, and industry-specific marketing techniques, as well as how AI is being leveraged in the marketing industry. Additionally, explores diverse career pathways in marketing, from established to emerging career paths. Explains foundational skills in strategic thinking, creative problem-solving, and professional communication that are valuable across numerous industries.

courses.title: Visual Storytelling: Crafting Stories Through Data & Design
courses.description: Introduces how to turn ideas, data, and personal experiences into compelling visual stories that grab attention and make a real impact. Explores how visual elements like color, layout, and typography work together to create powerful communication, while learning to use AI tools and digital platforms to enhance their storytelling. Examines how visual stories function across activism, marketing, journalism, and social media, while being sensitive to ethical and cultural considerations in visual communication. Explains the skills to become an effective visual communicator who can connect with audiences and create meaningful change.

courses.title: Future You: Leveraging AI for Success
courses.description: Provides first-year undergraduate applicants an overview of the current applications, trends, and overall impact of AI on business, industry, and society. Focuses on potential benefits and challenges of this new technology.

courses.title: Introduction to Healthcare Pathways and Professions
courses.description: Introduces healthcare systems, patient care roles, public health and medical innovations, and the business of healthcare. Explores foundational skills applicable to medicine, public health, allied health professions, healthcare administration, and related fields.

courses.title: Introduction to Engineering with Coding
courses.description: Introduces fundamental Python programming concepts, software development processes, and techniques for visualizing data through charts and graphs. Explores foundational skills applicable to technology, engineering, and related fields.

courses.title: Defending the Digital World: Cybersecurity Essentials
courses.current_step: Provost Continuing Education Module Oversight Group
courses.description: Explores cybersecurity fundamentals, digital safety, and career pathways in the cybersecurity field. Introduces content and prepares for future studies in information security, ethical hacking, cybersecurity analysis, digital forensics, and related technology fields while building essential digital citizenship skills for personal safety online.

courses.title: Working with Center for Research Innovation
courses.description: Explores three essential pillars that shape Northeastern's Center for Research Innovation's approach to research, innovation, and entrepreneurship. Topics include how research fuels invention, how Intellectual Property rights safeguard advancements, and how CRI's venture team extends an inventor's reach. Explores methodologies, strategies, and collaborations that enable Northeastern's Center for Research Innovation to translate the university’s most dynamic research into real-world results.

courses.title: AI in Action: Generate Your Experience
courses.description: Introduces the language and power of intelligent machines, how AI technologies can amplify professional growth, and how AI is applied to address real-world challenges across various sectors.

courses.title: AI in Action: Learn How to Put AI to Work for You
courses.description: Discover AI tools and their practical applications through interactive sessions with Northeastern Faculty and Staff. Gain insights into various tools, their related technologies, strengths, and limitations while having the opportunity to practice newly learned skills. Explore the process of building a custom bot, crafting AI prompts for research, and developing custom applications and data visualizations.

courses.title: Grow and Scale Your Business With AI
courses.description: Introduces AI technologies to drive business growth and scaling, with a focus on entrepreneurs and small businesses. Examines ways to apply AI tools to enhance productivity, streamline processes, and innovate strategies.

courses.title: AI Primer for Co-op Learning
courses.description: Introduces introductory concepts in responsible artificial intelligence (AI), including development, fairness and bias, privacy, human-centered design, and industry applications. Examines AI’s historical evolution, its impact across industries, and emerging trends. Explores ethical considerations and responsible AI use through case studies and hands-on interactions with generative AI tools. Prepares students to critically engage with AI technologies in academic and professional settings.

courses.title: Beyond the Game: Principles of Leadership for Athletes
courses.description: Explores purposeful, organized, and systemic engagement for lifelong learning. Provides time, space, and tools for learners to engage in a process of meaning-making and exploration to think critically about who they are, what they stand for, and what they will do in their next stage of life. Provides learners with opportunities to engage in hands-on training that introduces them to diverse corporate and civic leaders. This learning experience is designed specifically for NFL athletes.

courses.title: Essential Concepts of AI
courses.description: Introduces fundamental concepts of responsible and human-centered AI, integrating ethics, governance, and human perspectives into AI design. Explores AI’s role in business strategy and how practical applications of AI can solve problems and increase competitiveness. Emphasizes ethical considerations, regulatory frameworks, and the societal impacts of AI for making informed decisions about AI solutions that address real human needs but support ethical business practices.

courses.title: Alcohol Skills Training Program
courses.description: Explores the science behind alcohol and other drugs through a harm-reduction lens. Offers risk reduction strategies for those making the decision to drink. Discusses the latest research on alcohol as it relates to college students. Covers topics such as alcohol’s effects on decision making and signs of alcohol toxicity.

courses.title: Up2Us: Bystander Intervention
courses.description: Introduces bystander intervention as one of the most promising strategies for preventing sexual violence. Provides an opportunity for students to learn how to recognize situations that are potentially high-risk for violence and strategies for effective bystander intervention. Covers how alcohol can affect a person’s ability to give consent. Explores resource options and ways to support survivors of sexual violence.

courses.title: RESPOND: Trauma-Informed Response to Disclosures of Sexual Violence
courses.description: Examines trauma-informed approaches to supporting survivors of sexual violence. Explores impacts of sexual trauma, considerations for an effective response, and Northeastern resources. Reviews procedures for Mandatory University Reporters.

courses.title: Preventing and Responding to Sexual Violence in Student Organizations
courses.description: Focuses on preventing and responding to incidents of sexual violence within student organizations. Provides student leaders with an opportunity to learn about campus resources available for support, as well as to reflect on organizational culture and build skills to intervene and respond to allegations of sexual violence.

courses.title: RESTORE: Restorative-Informed Response to Accusations of Sexual Violence
courses.description: Focuses on educational frameworks for how to engage with people who have been accused of causing sexual violence or other forms of harm. Offers students an opportunity to learn about restorative justice and sexual harm, engage with examples, and learn about resources available to the campus community.

courses.title: Preventing and Responding to Sexual Violence in Fraternity and Sorority Life
courses.description: Focuses on the nuances of preventing and responding to incidents of sexual violence within the Fraternity and Sorority Life community. Provides FSL e-board members with the opportunity to build skills to intervene and respond to allegations of sexual violence, reflect on organizational culture, and learn about campus resources available for support.

courses.title: Neurodiversity Approaches and Inclusion
courses.description: Explores concepts of neurodiversity and its implications for education and employment. Introduces practical strategies for promoting inclusivity and harnessing the unique talents of neurodivergent individuals. Offers learners an opportunity to develop skills needed to foster environments where all members can contribute and succeed.

courses.title: Reflective Storytelling for Youth Workers
courses.description: Introduces Massachusetts Promise Fellowship youth workers to reflective storytelling as a method for documenting and sharing service experiences in ethical, meaningful, and practical ways. Engages participants in guided reflection, ethical storytelling practices, and interaction with basic program data to support the creation of a legacy artifact that captures insights, challenges, and lessons from a service year. Grounds learning in experiential learning theory and positive youth development principles to support professional growth and promote continuity and knowledge-sharing for future Fellows and partner organizations.

courses.title: Global Service Week
courses.description: Offers participants the opportunity to provide direct service to one of our committed community partner organizations across the Northeastern Global Network. Focuses on identifying community needs as defined by the organization and applying necessary action to address needs under the guidance of community leaders. Participates in critical reflection through purposefully selected activities.

courses.title: Community Volunteer Program
courses.description: Offers participants the opportunity to volunteer weekly with a local non-profit close to Northeastern in this experiential learning and co-curricular program. Focuses on skills in asset-based approach, interpersonal communication, and time management as part of this semester-long experience. Participates in critical reflection through purposefully selected activities.

courses.title: Global Serving-Learning Through Alternative Break Trips
courses.description: Offers students an opportunity to develop problem-solving and leadership skills through service-learning trips through the Alternative Breaks program. Engages with community organizations in the U.S. and around the world that address critical social issues, such as affordable housing, environmental conservation, food security, and public health. Explores places and cultures and working with people from different communities.

courses.title: Foundations of Business Learning
courses.description: Introduces primary functions of business such as organizational behavior, marketing, accounting, financial management, strategic planning, and innovation. Provides the opportunity to engage through readings, videos, and periodic learning check quizzes.

courses.title: Foundations of Software Engineering and Data Management Learning
courses.description: Introduces basic python programming functions, the software development lifecycle, big data, data management strategies, and data visualization. Provides the opportunity to engage through readings, videos, and periodic learning check quizzes.

courses.title: Foundation of Responsible AI Learning
courses.description: Introduces principles of responsible AI, including regulatory considerations, and analyzes how AI can enhance business strategies. Presents insights into integrating human perspectives into AI design through the lens of human-centered AI. Provides the opportunity to engage through readings, videos, and periodic learning check quizzes.

courses.title: AI and Media Strategy
courses.description: Explores the potential of artificial intelligence for strategic business opportunities. Describes cutting-edge and near-future revenue streams and business models being shaped by AI. Discusses actionable insights into emerging trends and technologies.

courses.title: Responsible AI For Leaders
courses.description: This immersive executive education course for business leaders is organized around seven sessions focused on navigating Responsible AI (RAI) practices effectively and building customer trust. Discusses driving RAI strategy and governance, building a culture to foster responsible innovation, ethical decision-making, and differentiation based on RAI strategies to create value.

courses.title: AI in Action: Build Your Toolkit
courses.description: Reviews trends in artificial intelligence and demonstrates how AI tools can be used responsibly to solve problems in the workplace. Examines AI applications in specific domain areas.

courses.title: AI in the Workplace
courses.description: Explores AI trends, AI tools, and how they can be used responsibly to solve problems in the workplace. Examines AI applications in specific domain areas.

courses.title: LEAD360 Undergraduate Leadership Program
courses.description: Discusses socially responsible and well-rounded leadership on campus and beyond. Explores the values of interdependence, accessibility, justice, and experiential learning through LEAD360 programs. Provides opportunities for students to evaluate the ways they collaborate and interact with others and identify the causes and topics that they are most passionate about.

courses.title: LEAD360 Husky Leadership Initiative
courses.description: Explores themes of personal development, student organizational leadership, accessibility, social justice, and collaboration through experiential workshops.Provides opportunities for students to better understand themselves and their personal leadership style.

courses.title: LEAD360 Graduate Leadership Institute
courses.description: Discusses socially responsible and well-rounded leadership on campus and in the workplace. Explores the values of interdependence, accessibility, justice, and experiential learning through the LEAD360 Graduate Leadership Institute program. Provides opportunities for students to evaluate the ways they collaborate and interact with others and identify the causes and topics that they are most passionate about.

courses.title: Venture Acceleration & Entrepreneurial Leadership
courses.description: Presents essential frameworks for building and scaling successful ventures. Introduces legal structuring, funding strategies, customer acquisition, operational excellence, and brand development. Provides opportunities to engage with founders, venture capitalists, and business leaders.

courses.title: AI Ethics & Digital Transformation Leadership
courses.description: Explores how leaders can drive responsible AI adoption and digital transformation within their organizations. Examines strategic frameworks for AI implementation, ethical considerations in algorithmic decision-making, and the philosophy of human-centered leadership in technological change. Provides opportunities to engage with technology executives, AI ethicists, and thought leaders.

courses.title: Leadership for Sustainability
courses.description: Examines complex sustainability challenges through systems thinking approaches and interdisciplinary methodologies. Explores analytical and critical thinking frameworks for addressing real-world environmental and social issues. Features partnerships with internationally recognized organizations and mentoring opportunities. Addresses stakeholder engagement strategies and collaborative problem-solving techniques. Offers students the opportunity to develop leadership capabilities and apply interdisciplinary knowledge to create demonstrable impact in sustainability contexts.

courses.title: Wet Lab Makerspace Community Project: Constructing Fluorescent Plasmids
courses.description: Introduces molecular cloning through Gibson assembly, a core technique in synthetic biology. Explores how genes encoding fluorescent proteins are cloned into plasmid backbones and transformed into E. coli. Provides hands-on experience with techniques such as PCR, DNA extraction, gel electrophoresis, and colony PCR, with opportunities to extend the workflow through advanced primer design.

courses.title: Peer Pathways Program
courses.description: Explores peer mentoring through theoretical and practical lenses. Examines graduate school as a multifaceted environment, emphasizing personal and professional growth. Focuses on mentoring best practices, including strategies for supporting and guiding new graduate students. Covers topics such as identity exploration, DEIB principles, promoting wellbeing, and leveraging alumni and peer networks. Offers opportunities to engage with resources, communities, and co-curricular activities that enhance graduate success and mentoring skills.

courses.title: Wellness Leadership
courses.description: Offers learners the opportunity to engage in a reflective journey to develop skills needed to understand and enhance personal and community wellness. Topics may include the wellness wheel, reflective practices, social/emotional literacy, and community engagement.

courses.title: Bridging Identity Literacy and Cultural Competence
courses.description: This 5-week course explores the intersections of identity literacy and cultural competence. Participants will gain a deep understanding of how these aspects of identity intersect and impact individuals' experiences. They will also develop practical skills to navigate diverse and inclusive environments effectively.

courses.title: Building Interview Communication Skills
courses.description: Explores language and communication skills for the behavioral interview stage of the hiring process. Introduces common types of behavioral questions, verb tenses for responding to questions, and the STAR model for structured responses. Provides learners with opportunities to develop their behavioral interview skills through classroom activities.

courses.title: Intercultural Competence in the Classroom and Beyond
courses.description: Provides learning opportunities on how to communicate interculturally with peers and instructors. Explores the foundations of effective communication, communicating across cultures, and the role intersectional identities play in communication. Introduces concepts of diversity, equity, inclusion, belonging, and reconciliation and how these play a role in communication practices with others. Develops intercultural competence and global understanding to face the complexities of our global reality.

courses.title: Mentorship and Leadership Experience
courses.description: Explores opportunities for professional and personal growth through campus-based learning and peer mentoring. Focuses on strengthening communication, networking, leadership, and intercultural competency. Offers interactive workshops, on-campus programs, and non-credit learning opportunities to enhance graduate success and practical skills development.

courses.title: Graduate Peer Mentor: Toronto Campus
courses.description: Explores the graduate peer mentor role through applied practice and facilitated reflection. Supports the development of communication, coaching, and interpersonal skills by engaging mentors in ongoing interactions with graduate students navigating Northeastern Toronto’s academic and social environment. Emphasizes leadership growth, problem-solving, and effective resource referral.

courses.title: Experience Expo
courses.description: Provides learners an opportunity to participate in interdisciplinary project teams to reflect, integrate, and apply technical skills and knowledge to solve real-world problems in industry projects. Explores and provides opportunities to practice professional skills, including collaboration, communication, public speaking, problem solving, and leadership.

courses.title: Experiential Network Professional Development
courses.description: Provides learners with the opportunity to engage with a workplace sponsor to successfully collaborate on and execute a workplace project to the sponsor’s satisfaction in a timely manner. Focuses on collaboration, problem-solving, project planning, and project deliverables.

courses.title: Introduction to Programming Taster Course
courses.description: Introduces fundamental programming concepts using the Python programming language. Explores probability theory and calculus principles essential for advanced computational work. Provides foundational mathematical and programming skills relevant to graduate-level study in computer science and related fields.

courses.title: Master's and Professional Doctorate Student Orientation
courses.description: Introduces Northeastern University’s resources and services such as Employer Engagement and Career Design (EECD), Global Learner Support, Office of Student Conduct and Conflict Resolution (OSCCR), Center for Student Engagement, etc. Provides students with the tools needed to navigate their graduate journey effectively, uphold academic and professional expectations, communicate professionally, and navigate their academic journey with integrity and respect.

courses.title: Graduate Greatness: Maximizing the Graduate Experience
courses.description: Explores strategies for personal and professional success in graduate education, including time and energy management, career development, and relationship-building. Examines ways to engage with university resources related to well-being, accessibility, and inclusion. Highlights tools for effective job searching and professional communication in digital spaces.

courses.title: Professional Skills for Academic Success
courses.description: Introduces core skills essential to all professionals, including task prioritization, time management, effective project management, stakeholder communication, and impactful presentations. Offers students the opportunity to develop skills for navigating the research lifecycle and leveraging professional networks for career advancement.

courses.title: Introduction to Community Service-Learning
courses.description: Introduces students to the fundamentals of community service-learning, focusing on the principles and practices of volunteerism. Offers students an opportunity to explore various social issues and the role of community organizations in addressing them. Provides opportunities for students to engage in introductory-level service activities.

courses.title: Intermediate Community Service-Learning
courses.description: Builds on introductory concepts by providing students with opportunities to develop and apply their skills in community service-learning. Offers students an opportunity to participate in multi-day service projects, working collaboratively to plan and execute community improvement initiatives. Emphasizes active engagement and skill development in areas such as event planning, teamwork, and project management.

courses.title: Advanced Application of Community Service-Learning
courses.description: Provides an in-depth exploration of community service-learning through sustained commitments to community organizations over a period of time. Offers students an opportunity to apply their knowledge and skills in real-world contexts, engaging in reflective practice and receiving feedback from community partners. Focuses on developing proficiency in community development, reflective practice, and non-profit communications.

courses.title: Working in Global Teams
courses.description: Discusses principles of team collaboration, cultural intelligence, and conflict resolution in diverse professional settings. Offers students opportunities to participate in interactive group activities, apply strategies for effective communication, and deliver persuasive presentations.

courses.title: GLS Writing Showcase
courses.description: Explores the intersection of academic, creative, and professional writing and various disciplines; the principles of academic integrity; and the practice of drafting, revising, and editing academic, creative, and professional writing.

courses.title: Speaking for Success
courses.description: Explores the role verbal communication plays in personal, academic, and professional success. Covers skills needed for small talk, presentations, and interviews.

courses.title: Academic Technologies Apprenticeship
courses.description: Prepares students in the Academic Technologies Apprentice Program to help support faculty with integrating and using technology to support teaching and learning. Provides comprehensive training in academic technology and digital pedagogy through hands-on projects including the development of an online course in Canvas. Offers students opportunities to establish a digital portfolio of work and to communicate pedagogical strategies to a network of colleagues.

courses.title: Multidisciplinary Research and Professional Development Skills for PhDs
courses.description: Introduces the professional skills needed to be successful in science PhD programs and beyond. Includes interactive workshops led by expert faculty and practitioners on critical PhD skills related to communication, research, entrepreneurship, ethics, teaching, and more. Covers how to apply critical thinking and problem-solving skills to a multidisciplinary research project. Offers students an opportunity to network with alumni, faculty, staff, and professionals to gain insights into the vast set of careers open to graduates.

courses.title: The PhD Entrepreneurship Immersion
courses.description: A one-week interactive program that engages participants in entrepreneurial thinking through skill development workshops, customer discovery exercises, and field study excursions to local startup companies in a variety of tech sectors. Taking part will provide the learner with entrepreneurial tools and frameworks, including identifying a high potential problem, customer interviewing, design thinking, pitching, and more.

courses.title: Biophysics Seminar
courses.description: Provides a valuable platform for students to engage with a broad range of topics critical to their development as researchers and professionals in biophysics. Covers research ethics, student work-in-progress presentations, and faculty-led introductions to biophysics research. Features guest lectures from external biophysics researchers, offering students exposure to cutting-edge developments in the field. Also focuses on career development, including grant writing, publishing, and building a scientific career in academia or industry. Offers students the opportunity to expand their knowledge beyond their own research focus, foster collaboration with peers and faculty, and strengthen their professional networks.

courses.title: Social Sciences and Humanities at Northeastern
courses.description: Intended for freshmen in the College of Social Sciences and Humanities. Introduces freshmen to the liberal arts in general. Offers students an opportunity to become familiar with their major, to develop the academic skills necessary to succeed (analytical ability and critical thinking), to become grounded in the culture and values of the university community, and to develop interpersonal skills—in short, to become familiar with all the skills needed to become a successful university student.

courses.title: Digital Methods for Social Sciences and Humanities
courses.description: Introduces programming skills and computational methods through application to topics in the social sciences and humanities. Methods include computational text analysis, network analysis, mapping software and analysis, computational approaches to data, Big Data, and/or social simulation. Offers students an opportunity to develop an understanding of the use and significance of computational tools for social sciences and humanities. No previous programming experience required.

courses.title: Bostonography: The City through Data, Texts, Maps, and Networks
courses.description: Uses Boston as a case study for integrating computational methods with the social sciences and humanities to provide new insights into major cultural, historical, and societal questions as they relate to and extend beyond the city of Boston. Through lectures, discussions, and labs, the course examines a variety of data sets that measure geographic, historical, literary, political, civic, and institutional landscapes. Offers students an opportunity to combine analytical tools, such as geospatial mapping, data visualization, and network science, with readings, hands-on class activities, and museum or site visits, enabling a comprehensive view of complex cultural and social phenomena.

courses.title: Information Design and Visual Analytics
courses.description: Introduces the systematic use of visualization techniques for supporting the discovery of new information as well as the effective presentation of known facts. Based on principles from art, graphic design, perceptual psychology, and rhetoric, offers students an opportunity to learn how to successfully choose appropriate visual languages for representing various kinds of data to support insights relevant to the user’s goals. Covers visual data mining techniques and algorithms for supporting the knowledge-discovery process; principles of visual perception and color theory for revealing patterns in data, semiotics, and the epistemology of visual representation; narrative strategies for communicating and presenting information and evidence; and the critical evaluation and critique of data visualizations. Requires proficiency in R.

courses.title: International Business Podcast Development and Production
courses.description: Offers students an opportunity to engage in an international business podcasting competition where an in-depth understanding of international business concepts such as multinational management, foreign direct investment, modes of entry into foreign markets, government-business relationships, globalization, and other topics are discussed. Includes topics such as interviewing, storytelling, A/V production and editing, as well as other technical aspects of creating a podcast.

courses.title: Understanding and Managing Cultural Differences
courses.description: Emphasizes the importance of cultural differences in international business. Describes the value of developing cultural agility in a global career, and discusses startups in diverse cultural contexts. Focuses on learning from international experiences and highlights the science behind what makes people successful in different countries and with people from different cultures. Discusses how to use this science to facilitate cultural agility development. Presents frameworks to understand cultural values; explains cultural curiosity and tolerance of ambiguity; emphasizes cultural responses such as adaptation, minimization, and integration; and studies living and working in a host country. Offers students an opportunity to develop self-management and relationship-management competencies in diverse cultural environments.

courses.title: Executing Global Strategy
courses.description: Emphasizes global strategy and the leadership requirements necessary to execute global strategy. Offers a capstone, “big picture” course that draws on and integrates all business fields and presents a global manager’s perspective. Uses the knowledge acquired in core courses—such as finance, accounting, operations, marketing, and organizational behavior—along with their international dimensions, to study how global managers reach strategic management decisions for the firm and its role in society. Offers students an opportunity to review and revise their professional development plans following their return from the expatriate experience and begin to develop post–Northeastern PDPs.

courses.title: Special Topics in International Business
courses.description: Examines areas of current interest and special topics in the field of international business. May be repeated once.

courses.title: Democracy, Authoritarianism, and Development
courses.current_step: INTL Undergraduate Department Curriculum Committee Chair
courses.description: Surveys the key challenges in the evolution of Democracy, Economic Development, and adaptive Authoritarianism since the end of the Cold War. Examines the geopolitical aftermath of the Cold War; democracy and development in the Global South; economic and political transition in the Former Soviet sphere across Eurasia; the spread of neoliberalism and the globalization of the world economy and its impacts; and the ways authoritarian regimes adapted and persisted despite internal and external pressure for liberalization and inclusion.

courses.title: Introduction to Cybersecurity
courses.description: Introduces foundational concepts, technologies, and practices in information security within modern organizational environments. Examines cybersecurity as an integral component of all IT domains, including networks, software, data, and emerging technologies. Explores the technical, managerial, legal, and ethical dimensions of cybersecurity, with emphasis on risk analysis, threat modeling, secure system design, secure network best practices, and security operations. Addresses global legal and regulatory frameworks, the business imperatives driving cybersecurity investment, and the evolving threat landscape shaped by the internet, cloud computing, and artificial intelligence. Through conceptual analysis and applied examples, students develop the knowledge necessary to evaluate security challenges and contribute to effective cybersecurity decision-making.

courses.title: Dante’s "Inferno" and Medieval Italian Culture
courses.description: Presents an overview of Dante’s "Commedia," with a focus on the first book, "Inferno." Explores the descending levels of hell, analyzing the historical and cultural context of Dante's time, including political events and social structures. Examines theological concepts such as sin, punishment, redemption, and the afterlife from the perspective of Christian theology. Through the literary analysis of selected chapters (canti), evaluates the potential relevance of the poem to the modern human condition and to the reader's own experiences. Taught in English.

courses.title: Advanced Reporting
courses.description: Continues JRNL 1101. This is the second writing course for undergraduate journalism students with an emphasis on learning how to report news stories. Offers students the opportunity to find sources and interview them, do background research, and use public records. Developing story ideas using computer-assisted reporting will be covered. Examines how to develop a story idea and then focus and organize it. Covers basic principles of online journalism including writing, design, and integration of visuals and text for the Web. Introduces elements of design and layout.

courses.title: Investigative Reporting
courses.description: Offers students an opportunity to learn and apply advanced reporting techniques of the kind that editors and producers expect of their best reporters, especially those who cover demanding beats such as politics, government, healthcare, education, science, and business. Studies how to see and apply data and data visualization techniques, to develop and interview sources, to locate and decipher public records, to identify and conceptualize important stories, and to discuss and apply ethical theories to reporting to justify choices that may inflame or antagonize sources or readers. An assignment to do substantial enterprise stories for publication in major media outlets is part of the course.

courses.title: News, Ethics, and the First Amendment
courses.description: Covers the rise of journalism as a skilled profession with codes of ethics and standards of conduct; the meaning of objectivity; and major First Amendment cases pertaining to libel, criticism of the government, censorship, protection of confidential sources, access to government information, and privacy. Examines the deontological and utilitarian theories of ethics and how they apply to journalistic practice. Introduces how technological change has altered the legal and ethical media landscape. Investigates how current events and court cases intersect with legal and ethical issues.

courses.title: Beat Reporting
courses.description: Covers advanced reporting in specific topic areas. Topics change from semester to semester. May be repeated without limit.

courses.title: Experimental Video Storytelling
courses.description: This course challenges students to break the traditional, decades-old model of video news storytelling by experimenting with new methods for telling stories with video. This will include producing short-form mini-documentary-style news pieces with animation and data visualizations. Students will create vertical video news stories with mobile-centric approaches designed to engage news consumers of all ages on digital platforms. Students will also design an audience research survey to compare traditional video storytelling with experimental methods through A/B testing, gather and analyze data, and draw conclusions on how experimental storytelling resonates with audiences.

courses.title: Introduction to Research Methods and Statistics in Social Science
courses.description: Designed to provide an understanding of quantitative research methods and basic statistics, students will learn how to use quantitative methods such as survey, experiment, content analysis, and computational methods to conduct research or journalist work. Students will learn how to collect and analyze data using appropriate descriptive and inferential statistical methods and be able to interpret the statistical analysis. This is a hands-on and lab-centric course which consists of a mixture of lecture and lab sessions designed to help master skills to design an appropriate methodology to test hypotheses and research questions and develop proficiency in using statistical software to analyze data.

courses.title: Topics in Podcasting
courses.description: Focuses each semester on a specific topic in podcast production, e.g. reporting a first-person narrative, perfecting the art of the interview, producing an audio documentary, building a daily news show or creating an influencer chatcast. Course topics explore podcast industry trends and innovations in storytelling. Informed by close listening, critical readings, instructor and guest lectures, class discussions and hands-on production work, students have the opportunity to develop and produce original podcasts. Emphasis is placed on practicing skills and strategies for both self-directed and collaborative podcast production, including experimentation, planning, iteration, revision, and critique of creative work. May be repeated twice for a maximum of 12 semester hours.

courses.title: Special Topics in Language
courses.description: Focuses on a particular theme of language and society that involves several languages (e.g., common literary themes, treatment of fairy tales, or folklore). The specific topic is chosen to reflect relevant comparative themes and expressed student interests. Requires an advanced level of skill in a language. May be repeated without limit.

courses.title: Civil Procedure
courses.description: Introduces the procedural rules that courts in the United States use to handle civil disputes. Focuses on the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure as well as federal statutes and constitutional provisions relating to the process of litigation. Covers topics such as federalism, processes of litigation, advocacy, and methods of dispute resolution.

courses.title: Constitutional Law
courses.description: Studies the techniques of constitutional interpretation and some of the principal themes of constitutional law: federalism, separation of powers, public vs. private spheres, equality theory, and rights analysis.

courses.title: Torts
courses.description: Presents an overview of the law of civil (meaning noncriminal) liability. Focuses on intentional torts such as assault and battery, the law of negligence, and strict liability regimes. Explores common law and common-law reasoning, and compares the common law as a tool for preventing injury and compensating for loss with public law (statutory and regulatory) approaches.

courses.title: Administrative Law
courses.description: Introduces the legal doctrines designed to empower and constrain government agencies and officials in their daily practice of governance. Topics include the constitutional status of administrative agencies, due process, the Administrative Procedure Act, and the availability and standards of judicial review of agency actions. Emphasizes the historical evolution of the modern administrative state and the regulatory agency’s role in our system of governance.

courses.title: Advanced Criminal Procedure: Adjudication
courses.description: Examines the procedural requirements and protections in the prosecution and defense of criminal cases in state and federal courts. Investigates how the law fashions the adjudicatory process and evaluates what is “fair” and “just” in the process. Covers right to counsel, pretrial detention, discovery, plea bargaining, trial processes, and sentencing, among other topics.

courses.title: Secured Transactions
courses.description: Surveys commercial lending transactions, with particular emphasis on Article 9 of the Uniform Commercial Code, consumer legislation, relationship to real estate mortgage law, lawyer's relationship to bankruptcy problems, fraudulent conveyances, and federal tax liens. Examines the legal framework governing secured and unsecured lending. Explores creditor rights, debtor protections, and priority disputes in commercial lending contexts. Analyzes the role of legal counsel in structuring transactions, conducting due diligence, and addressing potential complications in lending arrangements.

courses.title: Family Law
courses.description: Studies the key legal doctrines of the family law system in the United States. Explores regulation of intimate relationships including marriage, divorce, and the economics of dissolution. Examines the legal concept of the family, parental rights and responsibilities, custody, support, dependency, and the best interests of the child. Considers variations in state laws regulating families and the intersection of family law with other areas of law such as tax, immigration, and administrative law.

courses.title: Immigration Law
courses.description: Presents an overview of U.S. immigration law. Focuses on the day-to-day practice of immigration law, including the substantive and procedural aspects of this practice. Offers a historical perspective on changes in immigration laws and policies. Covers nonimmigrant and immigrant classifications, the preference system for immigrants, grounds of inadmissibility and deportability, relief from removal, asylum, citizenship, administrative and judicial review, and the immigration consequences of crimes.

courses.title: Prisoners' Rights Clinic
courses.description: Focuses on the law and procedures of parole, commutation, and prisons in Massachusetts. Involves representation of incarcerated people who are serving life-with-parole sentences at their parole release hearings or life-without-parole sentences at commutation hearings. These are public hearings conducted before the seven-member Parole Board or, in the case of commutation hearings, the Advisory Board of Pardons. Provides hands-on experience in criminal law and procedure, sentencing law, probation, prison classification and disciplinary systems, and often immigration law. Offers students an opportunity to develop and refine important advocacy skills including interviewing and counseling, case strategy development, thorough investigation techniques, witness preparation, and making strong opening and closing statements. Includes a seminar class, individualized supervision meetings, and direct casework.

courses.title: Trusts and Estates
courses.description: Covers all aspects of inheritance─intestacy, wills, common modern will substitutes, trusts, and future interests─focusing on rights of spouses and children, charitable interests, fiduciary duty, and other issues. Emphasizes the practical. Requires students to write numerous short exercises—including analysis, planning advice, and formal drafting—to address realistic problems.

courses.title: Federal Courts and the Federal System
courses.description: Studies the distribution of power between the states and the federal government and between the federal courts and other branches of the federal government as manifested in jurisdictional rules of the federal courts. Covers the structure and operation of the U.S. federal courts and the structures and doctrines that control what the Constitution calls “the judicial power of the United States.” Emphasizes the procedures and remedies involved in federal court claims against government officials at the national, state, and local levels.

courses.title: Domestic Violence Clinic
courses.description: Offers students an opportunity to represent survivors in family and domestic violence-based immigration law matters using a holistic, client-centered approach. Facilitates the development of traditional lawyering skills through interviewing and counseling clients and preparing and presenting cases in family and district courts in the Greater Boston area. Supports participation in a broader community-based response to domestic violence through training and advocacy with community organizations, medical providers, law enforcement, and court personnel.

courses.title: Labor Law 1
courses.description: Introduces the law of labor relations by examining the National Labor Relations Act and leading cases, in conjunction with historical, social, and economic materials. Topics include employee organization, union recognition, unfair labor practices, and collective bargaining.

courses.title: Professional Responsibility
courses.description: Focuses on the legal, ethical, and professional dilemmas encountered by lawyers. While not designed specifically to prepare students for the Model Professional Responsibility Examination, focuses on the American Bar Association’s Model Rules of Professional Conduct. Examines the social, economic, and political history of the profession and its current role in achieving social justice.

courses.title: Bioethics and the Law
courses.description: Explores the intersection of law, medicine, and ethics in diverse contexts that call for the evaluation of policies and practices implicating individual or public health. Analyzes the tensions between legal and ethical frameworks and considers how to address them. Focuses on bioethical and legal approaches in areas that may include end-of-life care, gender-affirming care, reproductive care, race-based differential outcomes in healthcare, and care of disabled patients, among other areas.

courses.title: Advanced Criminal Procedure: Investigation
courses.description: Examines the law governing police behavior, primarily by examining the constitutional doctrines of the Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Amendments that regulate law enforcement action. Topics may include search and seizure, stop and frisk, electronic surveillance, probable cause, warrants and their exceptions, police use of force, custodial interrogation, eyewitness identification procedures, and exclusionary remedies. Considers policy questions about the regulation, democratic accountability, and scope of police activity in the United States today.

courses.title: Employment Law—Compensation, Benefits, and Retirement
courses.description: Surveys legal, economic, and social policy issues concerning wages and working time, leave, unemployment insurance, and retirement income. Covers the Fair Labor Standards Act, the Family and Medical Leave Act, and the Unemployment Insurance program. Presents retirement and survivor income under the Social Security Act and pension regulation under the Employee Retirement Income Security Act. Examines problems faced by low-wage workers and women workers as well as tensions between the design of the older, statutory schemes and contemporary trends in business and work organization.

courses.title: Refugee and Asylum Law
courses.description: Explores humanitarian relief under the Immigration and Nationality Act including refugee resettlement, asylum, withholding of removal, and relief under the Convention Against Torture. Focuses on U.S. law as it has evolved since passage of the Refugee Act of 1980. Analyzes administrative and judicial case law from the United States and compares relevant international law and standards in other countries. Examines the administrative adjudication system with attention to issues including due process, cross-cultural communication, and trauma-informed lawyering. Discusses policy developments that impact asylum seekers in the United States.

courses.title: International Trade
courses.description: Introduces the legal framework for U.S. and international regulation of international trade. Includes a brief introduction to the economics of trade and trade restriction measures. Focuses on the World Trade Organization agreements regulating international trade in goods, services, and intellectual property; presents an overview of selected other trade agreements; and examines U.S. trade laws, particularly relief from “unfairly” traded imports, boycotts, and trade sanctions.

courses.title: Transactional Drafting Seminar
courses.description: Focuses on writing improvement within the context of transactional legal documents. Provides tools to achieve clear and concise writing. Identifies the purpose of each element of a contract and helps students develop language to accomplish that purpose. Offers students an opportunity to draft the operative provisions of a contract to express the agreement of the parties and demonstrates how individual contract provisions work together in a cohesive document. Addresses concepts applicable to a wide range of transactional legal documents, with emphasis on drafting in the context of corporate transactions in areas that may include employment, shareholder rights, and mergers and acquisitions.

courses.title: Civil Rights and Restorative Justice Clinic
courses.description: Introduces investigative research and restorative justice approaches that have brought some measure of redress to families and communities affected by lynching and other forms of racialized violence in the Jim Crow era. Offers students an opportunity to conduct factual investigations by tracking down materials from government repositories, newspaper archives, and court proceedings; collaborating with practicing lawyers, professional archivists, and historians; conducting interviews; and, where feasible, visiting the region where the events took place. Focuses on practical legal research, investigation, and writing skills. Designed to help students integrate the law of torts, civil procedure, federal courts, criminal law, human rights law, and constitutional law.

courses.title: International Business Transactions
courses.description: Introduces transnational commercial law. Discusses the legal framework for international sales transactions including the commercial terms of sales agreements, shipping contracts, insurance, financing arrangements, and customs documentation. Examines foreign direct investment transactions, international franchise and distribution agreements, and contracts for the transfer of technology. Includes materials on bribery of foreign officials and liability under U.S. and international rules. Briefly surveys dispute resolution issues, emphasizing choice of law and forum and arbitration.

courses.title: Drug Law and Policy
courses.description: Focuses on three domains of the broader subject of drug law: the evolution and current state of the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act; the architecture of the drug regulation system in the United States including the distinct space occupied by the Food and Drug Administration, the Department of Agriculture, and the Drug Enforcement Agency; and the role of regulation and tort litigation in harmonizing drug policy with science. Discusses legal and policy case studies.

courses.title: American Legal Thought: Traditional and Critical
courses.description: Surveys the intellectual history of U.S. law and practice, focusing on antiformalist and legal realist criticism of Classical Legal Thought (circa 1880 to 1940) and the fallout of that debate up to the present. Draws on students’ personal experience to examine American legal education and professional socialization. Explores whether legal work is a medium in which one can pursue projects oriented toward political and social change.

courses.title: Community Business Law Clinic
courses.description: Offers students an opportunity to develop lawyering skills through the real-world transactional representation of underserved community-based small businesses, entrepreneurs, and nonprofit organizations as they build strong legal foundations and create impact. Students take direct ownership of the legal representation and advise their clients on often-complex business legal issues. Includes an intensive seminar and close faculty supervision.

courses.title: Wrongful Convictions and Post-Conviction Remedies
courses.description: Analyzes legal and procedural safeguards designed to prevent wrongful convictions. Examines how the emergence of DNA testing has assisted law enforcement in solving crimes while also helping to expose the scale of wrongful convictions in the United States. Explores the primary factors that contribute to the phenomenon of wrongful convictions, the state and federal procedures through which post-conviction claims are litigated, and potential reforms to protect against the conviction of the innocent. Addresses the role of biological evidence, eyewitness identification, false confessions, and forensic science in criminal justice.

courses.title: Law Practice Management: Access to Justice
courses.description: Challenges conventional law practice management by exploring means and methods of filling the market gap in the provision of legal services to middle-class clients. Investigates and documents ways to use improved marketing techniques, staffing patterns, technological innovations, and a variety of other tools to provide legal services to underserved portions of the market in a sustainable and economically viable fashion. Requires students to conduct independent research to develop a law firm business plan, exploring a practice area of particular interest to them.

courses.title: Healthcare Fraud and Abuse Law
