Provides opportunity to develop necessary skills to monitor drug therapy in the ICU, integrate critical care knowledge and concepts, apply practice guidelines, and communicate therapeutic recommendations to ICU nurses and physicians. Designed for those with an interest in providing comprehensive clinical pharmacy services to critically ill patients in the ICU.

courses.title: Medicine Across Media: Healthcare Representations and Reality
courses.description: Examines how medical professions, patients, illnesses, and the healthcare system are portrayed across diverse media including printed materials, film, television, and social media. Analyzes representations of different health professions and clinical scenarios in popular culture to interrogate social and personal attitudes toward health, disease, and patient care. Explores how media shapes public understanding of healthcare, what stereotypes persist about medical professions, how patients are depicted, and whose stories get told. Develops critical medical literacy skills while examining real-world implications of these portrayals on the healthcare environment, including patient expectations, professional identities, and health policy.

courses.title: The Other Side of the Chart: Patients as Teachers
courses.description: Explores the essential role of healthcare providers as patient advocates and communicators through the lens of real patient experiences. Examines the differences between patient and provider perspectives as students self-assess and evaluate their active listening and empathetic counseling skills and build collaborative care competencies. Explores themes of illness, resilience, and healthcare navigation. Offers learners an opportunity to develop strategies to address medication-related challenges including polypharmacy; drug interactions; risk-benefit assessments; and systemic barriers such as insurance complexities, practice policies, and specialty pharmacy logistics through analysis of patient narratives. Applies the healthcare provider’s unique role to support patients through their complex health journeys, from routine medication counseling to life-altering diagnoses, while honoring diverse patient values, belief systems, and support networks.

courses.title: Biomedical Sciences and the Nanoworld Seminar
courses.description: Focuses on foundational cell biology, the applications of nanomedicine, and advanced therapeutics. Discusses contemporary concepts in cell biology and medicine in the context of health, disease, and preclinical drug development. Offers students an opportunity to design a novel nanomedicine, receive guidance on improving design, and present an elevator pitch evaluated by peers. Exposure to basic cell biology is recommended but not required.

courses.title: Biomedical Sciences and the Nanoworld Laboratory
courses.description: Offers hands-on opportunity to synthesize and characterize gold nanoparticles, conjugate a chemotherapeutic drug to the nanoparticles, treat human breast cancer cells with the nanomedicine, perform microscopy of the treated cells, and extract and analyze protein from treated cells. BioRAFT laboratory safety trainings are a prerequisite. Associated with Digital Badge: Biomedical Sciences and the Nanoworld Laboratory.

courses.title: Pharmaceutics 1
courses.description: Develops an understanding of pharmaceutical dosage forms, with emphasis on solids, liquids, semisolids, parenterals, inhalation, and novel drug delivery systems. Combines the discussion of pharmaceutical products developed in industry and those compounded in local pharmacies. Focuses on application of mathematical principles and problem-solving skills in pharmaceutical compounding.

courses.title: Foundations of Pharmaceutical Sciences 1
courses.description: Examines physical and chemical properties of drugs and discusses pharmaceutical products developed in industry and compounded in pharmacies. Focuses on application of mathematical principles and problem-solving skills in pharmaceutical compounding of dosage forms including solids, liquids, parenterals, inhalation, and novel drug delivery systems. Discusses thermodynamics, colligative properties, ionic equilibriums and buffers, solubility, protein binding, and reaction kinetics.

courses.title: Principles of Epidemiology in Medicine and Public Health
courses.description: Introduces the principles of epidemiology necessary to critically evaluate the published research in medicine, public health, and related fields. Through careful reading of literature, class discussion, and lectures, familiarizes students with the study of design-related considerations that are an indispensable part of interpreting scientific literature. Offers students an opportunity to learn how to recognize critical elements of research design and execution (e.g., loss to follow-up in randomized clinical trials and other cohort designs, selection bias in case control studies, etc.); identify the strengths and limitations of different approaches to research questions; deepen their understanding of causal inference; and recognize the provisional nature of scientific knowledge. Covers issues of statistical methods and data analysis; however, there are no computational requirements.

courses.title: Introduction to Scanning Electron Microscopy
courses.description: Reviews the fundamentals of the electron optics system and the signals produced during electron beam-sample bombardment in the SEM. Examines the principles of secondary electron (SE) and backscattered electron (BSE) imaging techniques as well as energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) to collect particular types of information about samples. Offers learners hands-on opportunities to operate an SEM and utilize these techniques independently. This course does not award academic credit.

courses.title: Physics for Engineering 1
courses.description: Covers calculus-based physics. Offers the first semester of a two-semester integrated lecture and laboratory sequence intended primarily for engineering students. Covers Newtonian mechanics and fluids. Stresses the balance between understanding the basic concepts and solving specific problems. Includes topics such as one-dimensional and three-dimensional motion, Newton’s laws, dynamics friction, drag, work, energy and power, momentum and collisions, rotational dynamics, forces, torque and static equilibrium, pressure, fluids, and gravity.

courses.title: Physics 1
courses.description: Covers calculus-based physics. Offers the first semester of a two-semester integrated lecture and laboratory sequence intended primarily for science students. Covers Newtonian mechanics and fluids. Emphasizes the underlying concepts and principles. Takes applications from a wide variety of fields, such as life sciences and medicine, astro- and planetary physics, and so on. Includes topics such as forces, torque and static equilibrium, one-dimensional and three-dimensional motion, Newton’s laws, dynamics friction, drag, work, energy and power, momentum and collisions, rotational dynamics, oscillations, pressure, fluids, and gravity.

courses.title: Physics 1 for Bioscience and Bioengineering
courses.description: Designed for students in engineering and science majors with a biologically related curriculum. Studies the fundamentals of calculus-based physics through a relationship with living systems. Includes topics such as kinematics of living systems, stress/strain/strength of biomaterials, fluid flow and boundary layers, aspiration and circulatory models, diffusion and random motion, and thermodynamics with examples from living systems.

courses.title: Foundations of Theoretical Physics
courses.description: Presents a comprehensive, self-contained introduction to the conceptual and mathematical foundations of theoretical physics. Commences with an integrated, first-principles approach to its five main areas: analytical dynamics, fields, statistical physics, relativity, and quantum theory. Focuses mostly on classical mechanics. Constitutes the first half of a two-semester calculus-based introductory physics sequence for science majors. Designed for students with preparation in Newtonian mechanics or those with advanced math preparation.

courses.title: Project Scope, Schedule and Cost Planning
Introduction to Program and Portfolio Management
Quantitative and Qualitative Methods for Business Analysis
Advanced Agile Product Delivery and Project Management
Agile Delivery for Sustainable and Resilient Organizations
courses.description: Focuses on enhancing organizational resilience and sustainability through the principles and methods of lean thinking, agile product delivery, and agile project management. Explores key aspects such as agile planning and execution, leadership, talent management, governance, and reporting. Offers students an opportunity to learn how to evaluate and improve organizational practices by applying practical, resilience-focused principles and methods effectively. Concludes with insights into release planning, portfolio reassessment, and strategies for measuring and enhancing organizational resilience.

courses.title: Quantitative Techniques
courses.description: Studies methods of quantitative analysis including descriptive statistics, hypothesis testing, cross-tabulation, analysis of variance, bivariate regression and correlation, and multiple regression. Examines how to generate and interpret statistical findings through use of Excel, SPSS, and/or other software programs. Uses examples from political behavior, public policy analysis, public opinion, comparative and international politics, and other areas of political and social-science inquiry to emphasize practical applications.

courses.title: International Development of the Global South
courses.description: Examines the major themes in development studies today. Explores approaches to the development and production, population growth, equity and poverty, rural and urban development, health and nutrition, education, and the international context of development assistance. Students considering a development administration concentration should try to take this course as their first in the field of development.

courses.title: International Political Economy
courses.description: Addresses international political economy and how we can understand the phenomenon of globalization. Offers a graduate-level introduction to the interaction between international politics and international economics in both industrial countries and developing countries. Introduces several theoretical approaches to international political economy and analyzes some of the classic issue areas of international trade relations, such as the international monetary and financial system; foreign direct investment and multinational corporations, debt, and development; the role of international political, economic, and financial institutions; and globalization.

courses.title: Government and Politics of the Middle East
courses.description: Examines the political and economic structures of the Arab states, Iran, Turkey, and Israel as well as inter-Arab politics and interstate conflict in the area. Emphasis is on Islam and politics, gender politics, and civil society.

courses.title: International Development of the Global South
courses.description: Examines the major themes in development studies today. Explores approaches to the development and production, population growth, equity and poverty, rural and urban development, health and nutrition, education, and the international context of development assistance. Students considering a development administration concentration should try to take this course as their first in the field of development.

courses.title: Public Diplomacy
courses.description: Introduces students to the theory and practice of public diplomacy in a global context. Public diplomacy is broadly understood as the ways in which governments, international organizations, non-state actors, and civil society project themselves to foreign publics in order to shape perceptions, build relationships, and advance their interests. Successful public diplomacy generates soft power and creates conditions favorable to the actor that engages in it.

courses.title: Principles of Learning
courses.current_approver_emails: a.aamer@northeastern.edu; m.boudreault@northeastern.edu; c.moffatt@northeastern.edu; m.saxena@northeastern.edu; c.bolick@northeastern.edu; k.thierer@northeastern.edu
courses.description: Presents the basic learning principles that permit humans to adapt effectively to a changing environment. Covers the science of how humans learn and the research and techniques of classical and operant conditioning with discussions of discrimination and generalization, biological constraints on learning, and other related topics. Relates learning principles to the understanding and treatment of behavioral, affective, cognitive, and motivational disorders.

courses.title: Quantitative Topics in Psychology and Behavioral Neuroscience
courses.description: Surveys key quantitative topics in psychology and behavioral neuroscience. Emphasizes theory and modeling, not statistics. Specific topics vary, but all are drawn from domains in which mathematics and computation play a significant role. Topics may include Fourier analysis with applications to vision and hearing, neural circuit computation, signal detection theory applied to human and nonhuman animals' decisions, measurement of sensory magnitudes in vision and hearing, the linear algebraic theory of color matching in humans, and analysis and models of human response time. Students complete weekly readings and a final project, typically involving use of software for data analysis.

courses.title: Lab for PT 3300
courses.description: Offers hands-on exploration of the human body utilizing cadaveric specimens and models. Covers the structure and function of the appendicular and axial skeletal systems of the body through prosected human cadavers and osteology. Emphasizes the skeletal, muscular, digestive, cardiopulmonary, and peripheral nervous systems.

courses.title: Interdisciplinary Seminar in Rehabilitation Science
courses.description: Aims to develop essential skills for analyzing, critiquing, and communicating scientific work across neurophysiological and behavioral neuroscience domains. Offers students the opportunity to offer and receive constructive feedback from international experts in a dynamic platform for scholarly exchange, as well as offer and receive feedback through student-led projects.

courses.title: Science Fiction and Film: Moral Dilemmas and Ethical Analysis
courses.description: Explores how science fiction films function as mythical cautionary tales about moral dilemmas of the twentieth and twenty-first centuries and as projections about how these dilemmas may be resolved or continue in the future. Provides a framework for an ethical analysis and examines how themes such as manifest destiny, nationalism, utopia, good vs. evil, war, and concepts of “the Other” are presented in classic and contemporary film. Also shows how science fiction film sometimes reinterprets pre-existing stories from world cultures and world religious traditions, updating earlier moral dilemmas to the contemporary situation.

courses.title: Artificial Intelligence (AI) in Regulatory Sciences
courses.description: Explores the rapidly evolving intersection of artificial intelligence (AI) and regulatory sciences, with a focus on how AI is transforming the development, approval, and regulatory oversight of human therapeutics.

courses.title: Fundamentals of Pharmacovigance for Trans-Atlantic Professionals
courses.description: Introduces the principles of pharmacovigilance through a comparative exploration of U.S. and European regulatory frameworks. Examines adverse drug reaction assessment, signal detection, risk management, and safety communications in global pharmaceutical contexts. Highlights distinctions between FDA and EMA approaches through case studies and regulatory analysis. Designed for professionals seeking foundational knowledge in international drug safety and regulatory affairs.

courses.title: Validation and Auditing of Clinical Trial Information
Clinical Trial Design Optimization and Problem Solving
Managing International Clinical Trials
courses.description: Focuses on initiating, collecting, and managing data from multicountry clinical trials and documenting the growing internationalization of clinical research in biomedicine. Focuses on trials carried out under the aegis of the U.S. Food and Drug Administration and investigators in the European Union, China, India, Africa, or Latin America regions. Studies the steps needed to conduct regulatory-compliant international trials using case studies and group projects.

courses.title: Pharmaceutical and Medical Device Law: Topics and Cases
Legal Issues in International Food, Drug, and Medical Device Regulation
Medical Device Product Development and Process Validation
Fundamentals of CMC Regulations and Methods
Medical Device Cybersecurity and Digital Health
Advanced Topics in Advertising and Promotion of Drugs and Medical Devices
Nonclinical Regulations in Biomedical Product Commercialization
Strategic Planning and Project Management for Regulatory Affairs
Real-World Evidence and Patient-Reported Outcomes in Regulatory Decision Making
Emerging Trends and Challenges in Regulatory Affairs
Biomedical Product Development: Regulatory Affairs in a Market-Based Setting
Introduction to Food and Drug Administration Pharmaceutical Regulation
courses.description: Presents an overview of current U.S. biopharmaceutical statutes and regulations. Offers students an opportunity to develop a strong understanding of biopharmaceutical regulatory affairs. Topics include the historical development of U.S. biopharmaceutical regulation, as well as an overview of biopharmaceutical product and clinical development, and applicable U.S. Food and Drug Administration approval processes. Also discusses the potential postmarket approval regulations for biopharmaceutical processes.

courses.title: Medical Device Development: A Regulatory Overview
Medical Device Quality Management System: Principles and Applications
Risk Management: Compliance and Processes
courses.description: Examines risk management practices and their impact on patient safety, product quality, and clinical effectiveness. Discussions include analysis of medical device risk management guidance documents, how medical device companies strive to ensure compliance, the responsibilities of regulatory professionals in risk management systems, and regulatory issues stemming from poor supplier performance and management. Evaluates risk management systems as they relate to particular categories of biomedical products manufactured in specific contexts, using case-based investigations and real-world examples. Offers students an opportunity to obtain the skills and knowledge they need to customize effective risk management methods within various global settings.

courses.title: Medical Device Product Development in Canada
Therapeutic Product Development: A Regulatory Overview
Global Impact of Electronic Common Technical Document (eCTD) Submissions
Project Management in Early Drug Discovery and Development
Global In Vitro Diagnostic Regulations and Submissions
Capstone: Practical Applications in Global Regulatory Affairs
courses.description: Offers practice utilizing practical and application-oriented exercises and discussions designed to translate global regulatory requirements for pharmaceutical, biologic, and medical device product commercialization into submission-ready documents and broadly applicable regulatory science solutions. Topics covered include creating submission-quality documents based on relevant statutes, regulations, and guidelines; completing medical device and biopharmaceutical product development Good Laboratory Practice documentation; conducting product testing; performing inspections; and effective utilization of GxP requirements. Summarizes the curricular content of the MS Regulatory Affairs program. Coursework consists of group and individual projects and research applicable to regulatory and industry information, as well as activities designed to aid in comprehending global regulatory issues.

courses.title: AI and Technology in Supply Chain Management
courses.description: Examines current technology and analytics capabilities and how they drive supply chains, from marketing to sourcing. Focuses on disruptive and transformational technology in an end-to-end supply chain such as Blockchains, 3-D printing, autonomous vehicles, digital twins, etc. Generative AI are studied, including how they are used and/or should be used. Course examines how leading organizations employ these technologies to meet strategic objectives, provide value to the business, and make decisions across supply chains.

courses.title: Managing Healthcare Supply Chain Operations
courses.description: Examines concepts and topics related to the design and management of operations and supply chains in the healthcare sector. Focuses on activities and functions such as managing operations processes, demand and capacity planning, inventory management, logistics, procurement, relationship management, information technology systems and innovations, and emerging trends. Introduces various tools and techniques that enhance effective management of operations and supply chain in healthcare organizations.

courses.title: Exploring Data Concepts & Applications
courses.description: Explores data literacy concepts and tools and examines creative and practical applications of data in professional and personal contexts. Focuses on using data to support informed decision-making and highlights the role of collaborative data work in fostering insight and innovation across diverse domains.

courses.title: Generative AI for Innovation Teams
courses.description: Investigates how generative Artificial Intelligence tools impact how people work and learn. Explores common misunderstandings of AI in higher education and ways that generative AI can improve workflows on teams. Examines use cases, AI tools, and ethical concerns and risks.

courses.title: Empowering Data Driven Decision Making
courses.description: Empowering Data-Driven Decision Making is a project-based course for defining problems and identifying data-driven solutions. The course explores various data types, sources, and their nuances through practical examples. Learners will enhance their decision-making capabilities by gaining valuable skills in communicating data needs, identifying suitable data solutions, and framing data for effective sharing. These skills will foster impactful outcomes in learners’ professional roles.

courses.title: Effective Communication for Collaborative Success
courses.description: Explores strategies for building trust, setting expectations, active listening, giving and receiving feedback, and conflict resolution. Equips learners with practical tools to enhance communication, foster collaboration, and navigate workplace challenges effectively.

courses.title: Foundations of Leadership
courses.description: Explores theoretical and practical aspects of leadership. Provides learners with the opportunity to engage in experiential learning through the development of a personal leadership plan.

courses.title: Data & AI Literacy in Action
courses.description: Explores data and AI concepts, tools, and applications across diverse professional contexts. Offers opportunities to examine relevant data and AI topics while considering their connections to professional roles. Encourages the extension of learning beyond individual development to foster broader institutional impact through practical application.

courses.title: Data Governance Leadership
courses.description: Introduces fundamental data governance principles and practices essential for responsible data stewardship in higher education environments. Examines university policies on confidentiality of records and information, student privacy rights under FERPA, and institutional data access protocols. Explores Privacy by Design principles for building privacy controls into systems and processes. Addresses data classification according to institutional guidelines and metadata management standards for documenting data products. Emphasizes practical application of data governance frameworks through real-world scenarios. Offers learners the opportunity to develop compliant data practices while supporting institutional decision-making and operational excellence.

courses.title: Tableau Site Administration & Advanced Analytics
courses.description: Explores advanced capabilities across three core Tableau components: Site Administration, Prep, and Creator. Examines comprehensive site administration practices including project organization, user permissions management, usage analytics monitoring, and technical troubleshooting for enterprise environments. Focuses on comprehensive data preparation solutions through Tableau Prep flows, advanced transformations, and automated processing workflows. Addresses sophisticated Creator techniques for complex data modeling, advanced calculations with Level of Detail expressions, and interactive dashboard design optimized for performance and usability. Emphasizes integration strategies across the Tableau ecosystem, security implementation, and governance frameworks for maintaining scalable analytics infrastructure in professional settings.

courses.title: Snowflake Platform Management & Administration
courses.description: Focuses on advanced Snowflake data platform management and administration concepts. Examines complex data loading techniques and migration workflows across various data sources. Explores comprehensive access management frameworks, role-based security implementation, and governance compliance requirements. Emphasizes proactive data maintenance strategies, retention policies, archiving procedures, and data quality management. Addresses enterprise-level challenges and troubleshooting methodologies essential for Snowflake data stewardship and platform administration responsibilities.

courses.title: Data & Analytics Fellow
courses.description: Integrates three core areas of enterprise data management: data governance, Snowflake platform management, and Tableau ecosystem use and administration. Explores fundamental data governance principles and practices essential for responsible data stewardship in higher education environments. Focuses on advanced Snowflake data platform management and administration practices. Explores advanced capabilities across three core Tableau components: Site Administration, Prep, and Creator. Emphasizes practical application through real-world scenarios across all three domains. Provides learners the opportunity to develop integrated data stewardship competencies essential for enterprise environments and institutional decision-making support.

courses.title: Designing Staff Development
courses.description: For subject matter experts interested in creating a targeted staff development plan for D’Amore-McKim School of Business. Introduces the five-phase approach to building learning experiences: analysis, design, development, implementation, and evaluation (ADDIE). Covers teaching asynchronously, writing learning objectives, applying practical delivery and evaluation methods, and creating opportunities for participant feedback while curating and utilizing technology.

courses.title: Data Reporting & Visualization
courses.description: Learners will engage with the steps necessary to approaching data analysis, including the purpose of data analysis, data wrangling, and cleaning of the data for interpretation. Learners will be exposed to analytic programs and experience processes to graphically represent dataset findings. Learners will explore data reporting and visualization, which requires filtering large datasets of unstructured information and provides data-driven insight into the decision-making process.

courses.title: Business Event Planning
courses.description: Learners will engage with instruction and activities on the basics of business event planning. Learners will be exposed to budgeting, customer service, marketing, room design, vendor and venue selection, A/V technology, and business sponsorship. Learners will be introduced to strategies on communication, event themes, envisioning spaces, and practicing diplomacy with senior leadership.

courses.title: Business Advising Essentials
courses.description: Presents critical skills and knowledge to business school academic advisors for effective developmental academic advising as aligned with the National Academic Advising Association (NACADA) advisor competencies. Emphasizes the tools needed to successfully implement developmental advising. Allows advisors to see their efforts as collaborative opportunities to teach, set expectations, and problem solve with the goal of empowering others through their development. Offers learners a well-rounded foundation for advising a wide variety of business students’ holistic needs with a focus on developing advisors’ professional confidence and competence.

courses.title: Developing Cross-Cultural Agility
courses.description: Empowers learners to cultivate essential cultural agility skills vital for success in today’s global workforce. Offers self-assessment and reflective experiences allowing participants the opportunity to enhance their cultural competence and appreciation of diverse perspectives, before adapting their behaviors in various contexts. Focuses on building trust and fostering meaningful connections across-cultural boundaries, equipping learners with the skills needed to collaborate effectively across cultures.

courses.title: Social Media Ambassador
courses.description: Exposes learners to skills in social media marketing, video production and editing, project management, and communications. Engages learners in community management, blogging, and creative content development while learning to build an impactful online presence. Enhances digital storytelling and project management skills, preparing students for professional roles in marketing, communications, and digital media.

courses.title: Teaching with AI in Business Education
courses.description: Explores the ethical and effective integration of artificial intelligence (AI) into business education. Offers a framework for understanding academic integrity, applicable policies, and appropriate use of AI tools. Introduces practical strategies for designing AI-aware syllabi, assessments, and learning activities. Provides curated resources and examples to support informed instructional decision-making across disciplines. Encourages reflection on transparency, student expectations, and faculty roles in an AI-augmented academic environment.

courses.title: Teaching Online in Business Education
courses.description: Showcases best practices and learning design models for online business education. Explores the online course teaching structure and process within DMSB. Introduces the roles and responsibilities of process stakeholders. Offers options and examples for customization within online courses, as well as opportunities to participate in online course readiness and professional learning community.

courses.title: Leveraging Universal Design in Learning in Business Education
courses.current_step: Provost Continuing Education Module Oversight Group
courses.description: Examines the core fundamentals of the Universal Design for Learning (UDL) Framework. Explores how the UDL Framework can be used to drive inclusive and accessible business education outcomes. Offers strategies for leveraging UDL within business education, and opportunities for learners to apply UDL principles to their own teaching. Encourages critical reflection related to inclusive teaching practices.

courses.title: From the Womb to the Neonatal Intensive Care Unit: An Interdisciplinary Approach to Feeding Treatment
courses.description: This course will equip students with an in-depth understanding of the complex issues surrounding premature infant feeding development and treatment with an interdisciplinary focus. Students will learn about the development of the senses in the womb and how being born too early can impact these processes. We will discuss the basics of infant feeding treatment as it pertains to bolus modifications and sensorimotor interventions from an interdisciplinary lens.

courses.title: Hearing Health: Hearing Screening Provider
courses.description: Focuses on the purpose of conducting hearing screenings for all ages, the procedural steps, and the recommendations for family and educational staff. Offers the opportunity for hands-on experience while working with members from the community and building interprofessional skills.

courses.title: Hearing Health: Aural Rehabilitation Provider
courses.description: Focuses on aural rehabilitation and tips and strategies to share with patients and their communication partners to decrease the challenges associated with hearing loss and repair communication breakdowns in social and work-related environments. Offers opportunities for hands-on experience working with adults who present with hearing challenges.

courses.title: Statistical Analysis in Sociology
courses.description: Offers students an opportunity to obtain knowledge and skills essential for understanding the theory and practice of social statistics commonly used in social research. Topics covered include the operationalization of abstract concepts; descriptive statistics; correlation; bivariate regression; central limit theorem; confidence intervals; hypothesis testing; and key concepts such as association, causation, and spurious relationships. Statistical software is used to complete assignments.

courses.title: Technology and Society
courses.description: Focuses on the social and political context of technological change and development. Through readings, course assignments, and class discussions, offers students an opportunity to learn to analyze the ways that the internet, artificial intelligence, and other technological advances have required a reworking of every human institution—both to facilitate the development of these technologies and in response to their adoption.

courses.title: Latin American Literature
courses.description: Offers an overview of the major trends in Latin American narrative, poetry, drama, and essays, from Bernal Diaz through Borges and Bolaño. Studies broad cultural and political contexts, especially the Cold War period and the impact of neoliberalism. Conducted in Spanish.

courses.title: Multinational Corporations and Global Sustainability: Complexities, Strategies, and Societal Impact
courses.description: Explores the role of multinational corporations and their leaders in addressing key sustainability challenges including climate change, wealth inequality, and human rights concerns. Examines how multinationals, with their vast resources and global influence, are uniquely positioned to address global sustainability issues through management practices and actionable strategies. Investigates how national rules, norms, and cultural beliefs shape the responses of multinationals and influence their ability to drive positive change in society.

courses.title: Gentrification and Strategies of Community Preservation
courses.description: Delves into effective strategies employed by communities and city planners to mitigate the adverse impacts of gentrification. Explores the root causes and repercussions of gentrification, along with community-driven solutions and policy interventions. Emphasizes the intersection of race and the contentious relationship between gentrification and displacement. Addresses various forms of stratification associated with gentrification including class inequality, escalating housing costs, homelessness, and their interconnectedness with market forces and urban planning. Scrutinizes urban decline and redevelopment within a broader structural context, and compares the gentrification process in numerous cities across the United States and globally. Accentuates entrepreneurial approaches to addressing these issues.

courses.title: Mobile Wireless Communications and Networking
courses.description: Studies communications and networking issues in providing broadband wireless access to mobile applications. Discusses networking technologies required by converged IP-based applications. Covers converged network architectures and the interworking of different generations of access technologies with the Evolved Packet Core (EPC). Registration limited and by application only; it is expected that all students have prior knowledge of digital communications, radio propagation, cellular networks, and second-generation wireless standards.

courses.title: Technology Leadership Challenge Project 1
courses.description: Offers students an opportunity to develop and present a plan for the demonstration of a marketable technology product or prototype. This course is the first half of a thesis-scale project in technology commercialization. Requires work/training with a sponsoring organization or employer to improve a process or develop a project that is of significant value to the organization and demonstrates a quantifiable market impact while enhancing the student’s technological depth and fostering the student’s leadership development.

courses.title: Stagecraft
courses.description: Introduces students to the theoretical concepts, knowledge, skills, and responsibilities associated with a wide variety of stage technologies and activities to be found in the world of technical theatre. Combines a grounding in the history and theory of technical theatre practices with laboratory experience to offer students an opportunity to gain a fundamental understanding of key areas of production such as light and sound operation, scenic carpentry, properties, light hang and focus, organization of running crews, rigging, wardrobe crew, and costume construction. Addresses issues concerning stage safety, as well as imperatives of public assembly and fire codes. Requires 60 hours of practical laboratory work in the running of either a mainstage or Box Theatre production in addition to weekly classes.

courses.title: Script Analysis
courses.description: Focuses on in-depth analysis of theatrical structures of scripts primarily from the viewpoints of the actor, director, and designer. Emphasizes developing the student’s ability to synthesize the intellectual and intuitive work required to create a theatrical experience from a written text. Identifies and explores physical, thematic, musical, and abstract structures. Through critical thinking and analysis of plays and essays from a diverse and inclusive reading list, offers students an opportunity to develop a point of view and an informed perspective on both contemporary and canonical work, as well as a thorough understanding of beat analysis and the working vocabulary of actors. Requires secondary readings and several short papers.

courses.title: Scenic Painting and Treatments
courses.description: Explores the skills and techniques needed to execute theatrical painting. Historical and contemporary techniques and practices include the mixing of various paints and colors, brush selection and attributes, specific applications of paint for effect, and the proper use of additives. Considers techniques such as appliqué and textures, faux finishes, ornamental details, foliage, and aging and distressing. Studies primary and secondary research sources. Emphasizes the safe use of paint and treatment products. Requires an additional two and a half lab hours each week, which feature projects that make use of current industry practices and enhance the development of speed and accuracy in execution.

courses.title: Theatre Histories 1: Early Evidence Through 1750
courses.description: Examines trends in performance and theatrical production beginning with the earliest evidence and concluding at around 1750. Uses a global perspective to study many elements of theatre and performance including environments, architecture, scene and costume design, acting styles, textual and nontextual performance scripts, theory, and audiences. Considers the specific social and cultural conditions out of which these conventions emerged. Examines a wide range of sources and dramatic texts; engaging canonical pieces; as well as works that amplify the voices and stories of Black, Indigenous, people of color, women-identified, LGBTQIA+, neurodiverse, and disabled people and communities. Challenges students to recognize that history is never singular and that multiple histories of theatre necessarily coexist.

courses.title: Theatre Histories 2: 1750 Through Tomorrow
courses.description: Examines trends in performance and theatrical production beginning in the mid-18th century and continuing to the present, with perspectives on future developments. Uses a global perspective to study many elements of theatre and performance including environments, architecture, scene and costume design, acting styles, textual and nontextual performance scripts, theory, and audiences. Considers the specific social and cultural conditions out of which these conventions emerged. Examines a wide range of sources and dramatic texts; engaging canonical pieces; as well as works that amplify the voices and stories of Black, Indigenous, people of color, women-identified, LGBTQIA+, neurodiverse, and disabled people and communities. Challenges students to recognize that history is never singular and that multiple histories of theatre necessarily coexist.

courses.title: Stage Management: Concepts and Practices
courses.description: Studies the role of the stage manager and the essentials of stage-managing a production in the preproduction and rehearsal processes. Covers the role of the stage manager; communication, organizational, and production skills; the development of interpersonal and problem-solving techniques required in the production process; methods of blocking notation; and director/stage manager collaboration. Readings and discussions support paper projects and other production assignments. Requires 60 additional hours of lab time.

courses.title: Breaking the Glass Ceiling: Women in Theatre
courses.description: Surveys a wide range of dramatic forms and theatrical techniques pioneered by women and gender nonconforming theatre makers to reveal larger social issues. Examines how sociocultural contexts in a text represent diverse views of identity including cultural, ethnic, racial, and geographical experiences. Includes feminist theory and supplemental essays. Identifies how female and gender nonconforming artistic leaders, directors, designers, and playwrights have been historically perceived. Analyzes why the issue of gender equity in theatre remains unresolved.

courses.title: Directing for the Stage
courses.description: Focuses on purposes and techniques of theatrical direction related to script analysis, production style, pictorial composition, rhythmic evolution, and empathic responses.

courses.title: Scenic Design 3
courses.description: Continues THTR 4020. Emphasizes research and the skills needed to communicate a design concept to a director, stage manager, and scenic shop. Projects and venues are varied and offer students an opportunity to learn to do thumbnail sketches, color renderings, models, set plans, sections, elevations, and paint elevations. Covers the history of scenic design, including the latest advances in theatre technology.

courses.title: Special Topics in Acting
courses.description: Builds on advanced theoretical concepts and foundational skills acquired in prior study. Offers students an opportunity to choose further study to inform their individual development as actors. Topics are offered on a rotating basis and focus on a specialized aspect of acting technique. Incorporates advanced research and dramatic analysis. May be repeated once, but the topic may not be repeated.

courses.title: Breaking the Glass Ceiling: Women in Theatre
courses.description: Surveys a wide range of dramatic forms and theatrical techniques pioneered by women and gender nonconforming theatre makers to reveal larger social issues. Examines how sociocultural contexts in a text represent diverse views of identity including cultural, ethnic, racial, and geographical experiences. Includes feminist theory and supplemental essays. Identifies how female and gender nonconforming artistic leaders, directors, designers, and playwrights have been historically perceived. Analyzes why the issue of gender equity in theatre remains unresolved.
